<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hopfield Network (Definition):** A *Hopfield Network* is a single-layer, fully-connected, **recurrent (feedback) neural network** with symmetric synaptic weights ($w_{ij} = w_{ji}$), zero self-connections ($w_{ii} = 0$), and binary bipolar threshold neurons ($\{-1, +1\}$) that operates as a **Content-Addressable Memory (CAM)** / **associative memory** system. It was proposed by **John J. Hopfield (1982)** and is mathematically grounded in the **Ising model of statistical mechanics**.

A Hopfield network has two principal operational modes:
1. **Storage Phase** — Learning the weight matrix from a set of *fundamental (prototype) memories* using the **Hebbian outer-product rule**.
2. **Recall Phase** — Iterative asynchronous (or synchronous) updating of neuron states until the network converges to a **stable fixed point** (an *attractor*) that corresponds to the *closest* stored memory to the corrupted/partial input cue.

| Variant | Neuron State Space | Time Behaviour |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Discrete Hopfield Network (DHN)** | Bipolar binary $\{-1, +1\}$ | Asynchronous / synchronous discrete updates |
| **Continuous Hopfield Network (CHN)** | Continuous $u_i \in [0, 1]$ via sigmoid activation | Continuous-time, described by **Hopfield's differential equations (1984)** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Marble-in-a-Landscape" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — Memory as a Valley, Recall as a Ball Rolling Downhill:**
> Imagine a hilly terrain where every valley is a **stored memory** and the depth of each valley represents how *strongly* that memory is imprinted. The hills (local maxima) are unwanted transient states. When you present a *partial / noisy* input, you essentially place a **marble** on the landscape close to the correct valley. **Gravity (the energy-minimization dynamics) pulls the marble downhill** until it settles in the nearest valley. The valley it settles into is the *recalled* memory. This is the geometric essence of Hopfield's energy function $E$.

This analogy beautifully explains three key phenomena:
- **Convergence** — the marble must stop somewhere (the network always reaches a stable state).
- **Associative Recall** — even a *noisy* cue finds the right valley (pattern completion).
- **Spurious States** — some valleys are not real memories but "ghost" minima created by overlap between stored patterns (the marble can get trapped in a fake valley).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Bipolar neuron states**: $s_i \in \{-1, +1\}$
- **Asynchronous update probability**: One neuron updated per time-step
- **Storage capacity (Amit–Gutfreund–Sompolinsky bound, 1987)**: $p_{\max} \approx 0.138 \, N$ patterns for an $N$-neuron network
- **Network connectivity**: $N(N-1)/2$ independent weights (due to symmetry and zero diagonal)
- **Convergence time**: Finite, bounded by $\mathcal{O}(N)$ asynchronous steps in the worst case

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Network Architecture & Operational Constraints

The Hopfield network is governed by **four strict architectural axioms** (essential for KTU problems):

- **Fully Recurrent Topology** — Every neuron is connected to every other neuron (complete graph $K_N$).
- **Symmetric Weights** — $w_{ij} = w_{ji} \quad \forall\, i \neq j$. This guarantees the existence of a *Lyapunov energy function*.
- **No Self-Connections** — $w_{ii} = 0 \quad \forall\, i$. The diagonal of the weight matrix is identically zero.
- **Bipolar Binary States** — Each neuron $i$ outputs $s_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ (or sometimes $\{0, 1\}$ in older texts).

## 2.2 Operational Phases — Step-by-Step Logic

### Phase A: Storage (One-shot Hebbian Learning)
- **Why?** We need a weight matrix $W$ such that the desired patterns $\xi^{\mu}$ become *fixed points* of the dynamics.
- **How?** Use the **outer-product (Hebbian) rule**:
$$w_{ij} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{\mu=1}^{p} \xi_i^{\mu} \xi_j^{\mu}, \quad i \neq j$$
- **Why divide by $N$?** Normalization prevents weight magnitude explosion and stabilizes the energy landscape.
- **Matrix form**:
$$W = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{\mu=1}^{p} \boldsymbol{\xi}^{\mu} (\boldsymbol{\xi}^{\mu})^{T} - \frac{p}{N} I$$
where the $I$ term enforces the $w_{ii} = 0$ constraint.

### Phase B: Recall (Asynchronous Iterative Update)
1. **Initialize** the state vector $\mathbf{s}(0)$ with the input cue (noisy/partial pattern).
2. **Repeat** until convergence (no state changes in a full pass):
   - Pick a neuron $i$ at random (asynchronous) or update all (synchronous).
   - Compute the **local field** (postsynaptic potential):
$$h_i(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} w_{ij} \, s_j(t)$$
3. **Apply the signum threshold rule**:
$$s_i(t+1) = \text{sgn}\!\left(h_i(t)\right) = \begin{cases} +1, & \text{if } h_i(t) \geq 0 \\ -1, & \texttext{if } h_i(t) < 0 \end{cases}$$
4. **Convergence criterion**: When $\text{sgn}(h_i) = s_i$ for every neuron, the network is at a **stable fixed point**.

---

## 2.3 The Hopfield Energy (Lyapunov) Function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hopfield Energy Function** is the cornerstone of the model's stability proof. It is mathematically equivalent to the **Hamiltonian of an Ising spin system**:
$$E = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} w_{ij} s_i s_j$$
The dynamics **monotonically decrease** $E$ (or leave it unchanged), and since the state space is finite ($\pm 1$ for $N$ neurons gives $2^N$ states), the network **must converge in finite time**.

**Why does $E$ decrease?** Consider a state flip $s_k \to -s_k$. The change in energy is:
$$\Delta E_k = -2 s_k \sum_{j \neq k} w_{kj} s_j = -2 s_k h_k$$
If we flip according to $\text{sgn}(h_k)$, then $s_k h_k > 0$, so $\Delta E_k < 0$. Hence $E$ strictly decreases.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Key Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Energy Function** | $E = -\tfrac{1}{2} \sum_{i}\sum_{j} w_{ij} s_i s_j$ | Bounded below; converges to fixed point |
| 2 | **Local Field** | $h_i = \sum_{j} w_{ij} s_j$ | Net input to neuron $i$ |
| 3 | **Update Rule (Async)** | $s_i \leftarrow \text{sgn}(h_i)$ | Picks one neuron at a time |
| 4 | **Hebbian Weight** | $w_{ij} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{\mu=1}^{p} \xi_i^{\mu} \xi_j^{\mu},\; i \neq j$ | Outer-product learning |
| 5 | **Matrix Form** | $W = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{\mu} \boldsymbol{\xi}^{\mu}(\boldsymbol{\xi}^{\mu})^{T} - \frac{p}{N}I$ | Identity enforces $w_{ii}=0$ |
| 6 | **Stability Condition** | $\text{sgn}(h_i^{\mu}) = \xi_i^{\mu}\;\; \forall i$ | Pattern $\mu$ is a fixed point |
| 7 | **Storage Capacity** | $p_{\max} \approx 0.138\,N$ | Amit–Gutfreund–Sompolinsky (1987) |
| 8 | **Symmetry Constraint** | $w_{ij} = w_{ji}$ | Required for $E$ to be a valid Lyapunov function |
| 9 | **No Self-Loop** | $w_{ii} = 0$ | Prevents trivial self-reinforcement |
| 10 | **Energy Change on Flip** | $\Delta E_k = -2 s_k h_k$ | Always $\leq 0$ under signum rule |
| 11 | **Continuous Form (CHN)** | $C_i \tfrac{du_i}{dt} = -\tfrac{u_i}{R_i} + \sum_j w_{ij} v_j + I_i$ | $v_i = g(u_i) = \tanh(\lambda u_i)$ |
| 12 | **Number of States** | $2^N$ | Finite → guaranteed convergence |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pattern Completion & Noise Filtering** — Restoring corrupted images, fingerprints, signatures.
- **Optimization (Continuous Hopfield)** — Solving **NP-hard problems** like **TSP (Traveling Salesman Problem)**, **VLSI routing**, **job-shop scheduling**, and **analog-to-digital conversion** (the famous Tank–Hopfield ADC, 1986).
- **Associative Database / Cache** — Fast lookup by content rather than address.
- **Biophysical Modeling** — Theoretical bridge to understanding biological neural memory (long-term potentiation).
- **Combinatorial Search Heuristics** — Used as a building block in **Boltzmann Machines** and **Restricted Boltzmann Machines** for deep learning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Convergence Proof via the Energy Function

We must show that $E$ is monotonically non-increasing under the asynchronous update rule.

Let the network have state $\mathbf{s} = (s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_N)$. Suppose we flip the state of neuron $k$ from $s_k$ to $-s_k$, leaving all other neurons unchanged. The energy before the flip is:
$$E_{\text{old}} = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i}\sum_{j} w_{ij} s_i s_j$$
Because the network is symmetric ($w_{ij} = w_{ji}$) and $w_{ii} = 0$, we can regroup the double sum. The energy contribution involving neuron $k$ is:
$$E_{\text{old}}^{(k)} = - s_k \sum_{j \neq k} w_{kj} s_j = - s_k \, h_k$$
After the flip $s_k \to s_k' = -s_k$, the new energy contribution from neuron $k$ becomes:
$$E_{\text{new}}^{(k)} = - s_k' \sum_{j \neq k} w_{kj} s_j = + s_k \, h_k$$
Since all other terms (not involving $k$) are unchanged, the energy change is:
$$\Delta E = E_{\text{new}} - E_{\text{old}} = (+ s_k h_k) - (- s_k h_k) = -2 s_k h_k$$
Now, the update rule says $s_k' = \text{sgn}(h_k)$. Therefore:
- If $h_k > 0$: $s_k' = +1$, and since $h_k > 0$, the new $s_k' h_k > 0$, so $\Delta E < 0$. ✓
- If $h_k < 0$: $s_k' = -1$, and $s_k' h_k > 0$ as well, so $\Delta E < 0$. ✓
- If $h_k = 0$: $s_k' = s_k$ (no change), so $\Delta E = 0$. ✓ (stasis)

In all cases, $\Delta E \leq 0$. Since $E$ is bounded below (minimum is $E_{\min} = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i \neq j} \vert w_{ij} \vert$) and takes values on a finite discrete set, the network **must reach a stable fixed point in at most $2^N$ steps**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hebbian Storage of a Single Pattern

Suppose we want a single pattern $\boldsymbol{\xi} = (\xi_1, \xi_2, \ldots, \xi_N)$ to be a stable fixed point. We need $\text{sgn}(h_i) = \xi_i$ for every neuron $i$.

Set the weight using the outer-product rule:
$$w_{ij} = \frac{1}{N} \xi_i \xi_j \quad (i \neq j)$$
Compute the local field at neuron $i$ when the network is exactly in state $\boldsymbol{\xi}$:
$$h_i^{\xi} = \sum_{j \neq i} w_{ij} \xi_j = \sum_{j \neq i} \frac{1}{N} \xi_i \xi_j \cdot \xi_j = \frac{\xi_i}{N} \sum_{j \neq i} \xi_j^2$$
Because $\xi_j^2 = 1$ for bipolar patterns:
$$h_i^{\xi} = \frac{\xi_i}{N} (N - 1) = \xi_i \left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right) \approx \xi_i$$
So $h_i^{\xi}$ has the same sign as $\xi_i$, confirming $\text{sgn}(h_i^{\xi}) = \xi_i$. The pattern is stable. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hebbian Storage of $p$ Patterns (with Crosstalk Analysis)

For $p$ patterns, the weight is:
$$w_{ij} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{\mu=1}^{p} \xi_i^{\mu} \xi_j^{\mu}$$
The local field at neuron $i$ when probing pattern $\nu$ is:
$$h_i^{\nu} = \sum_{j \neq i} w_{ij} \xi_j^{\nu} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j \neq i} \sum_{\mu=1}^{p} \xi_i^{\mu} \xi_j^{\mu} \xi_j^{\nu}$$
Regroup the summations:
$$h_i^{\nu} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{\mu=1}^{p} \xi_i^{\mu} \sum_{j \neq i} \xi_j^{\mu} \xi_j^{\nu}$$
Split the inner sum into the desired signal term ($\mu = \nu$) and the crosstalk term ($\mu \neq \nu$):
$$h_i^{\nu} = \frac{1}{N} \xi_i^{\nu} (N - 1) + \frac{1}{N} \sum_{\mu \neq \nu} \xi_i^{\mu} \sum_{j \neq i} \xi_j^{\mu} \xi_j^{\nu}$$
The first term is the **signal** $\approx \xi_i^{\nu}$. The second term is **crosstalk noise** $C_i^{\nu}$:
$$C_i^{\nu} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{\mu \neq \nu} \xi_i^{\mu} \sum_{j \neq i} \xi_j^{\mu} \xi_j^{\nu}$$
For random unbiased patterns, $C_i^{\nu}$ behaves like a **Gaussian random variable with mean 0 and standard deviation $\sqrt{(p-1)/N}$**. The pattern $\nu$ is recalled correctly as long as:
$$\left\vert \xi_i^{\nu} \right\vert > \left\vert C_i^{\nu} \right\vert \implies 1 > \sqrt{\frac{p-1}{N}} \implies p < N$$
This heuristic gives a critical capacity $p_c \sim N$. A more rigorous analysis by **Amit, Gutfreund & Sompolinsky (1987)** using replica theory gives the famous bound:
$$p_{\max} \approx 0.138\,N$$
Beyond this, the system enters a **spin-glass phase** dominated by spurious states. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy of a Stored Pattern (Stability Margin)

The energy of pattern $\nu$ is:
$$E^{\nu} = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i}\sum_{j} w_{ij} \xi_i^{\nu} \xi_j^{\nu} = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i} \xi_i^{\nu} h_i^{\nu}$$
Substituting the signal + crosstalk decomposition:
$$E^{\nu} = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i} \xi_i^{\nu} \left[ \xi_i^{\nu}\left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right) + C_i^{\nu} \right]$$
$$E^{\nu} = -\frac{1}{2} \left[ (N-1) + \sum_{i} \xi_i^{\nu} C_i^{\nu} \right]$$
For large $N$ with random patterns, the second term averages to 0, giving:
$$E^{\nu} \approx -\frac{N}{2}$$
The **energy gap** between a stored pattern and a spurious state determines recall robustness.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Pattern-by-Pattern Decision Logic

| Pattern Status | Local Field Sign | Network Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Stored pattern** | $\text{sgn}(h_i) = \xi_i^{\mu}$ | Fixed point — no update |
| **Spurious state (mixture)** | $\text{sgn}(h_i) = \zeta_i$ (neither pattern) | Trapped in false minimum |
| **Reverse state** | $h_i \approx -\xi_i^{\mu}$ | Stable reversed memory |
| **Spin-glass state** | Inconsistent sign | Random, high-energy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):** For $N = 4$ and one pattern $\xi = (+1, -1, +1, -1)$:
> $$W = \frac{1}{4}\begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 & +1 & -1 \\ -1 & 0 & -1 & +1 \\ +1 & -1 & 0 & -1 \\ -1 & +1 & -1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$
> Applying the cue $(+1, -1, +1, -1)$ recovers $\xi$ in **zero steps** (already a fixed point). Applying the noisy cue $(+1, +1, +1, -1)$ produces $h = (0.5, -1.5, 0.5, -1.5)$, hence $\text{sgn}(h) = (+1, -1, +1, -1)$ — corrected in **one step**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hopfield Network Architecture (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["Input Cue X0"] --> initA["Initialize State Vector S"]
    initA --> pickA["Pick Neuron i Randomly"]
    pickA --> fieldA["Compute Local Field h_i = Sum w_ij s_j"]
    fieldA --> ruleA{"sgn h_i >= 0 ?"}
    ruleA -->|Yes| setP["Set s_i = +1"]
    ruleA -->|No| setN["Set s_i = -1"]
    setP --> checkA{"All Neurons Stable ?"}
    setN --> checkA
    checkA -->|No| pickA
    checkA -->|Yes| outputA["Output Final Memory S_final"]
```

## 4.2 Energy Landscape with Attractor Basins

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph energyLandscape["Energy Landscape E versus State Space"]
        direction TB
        peakA["Local Maximum E_high"] --> valleyA["Basin 1 - Stored Memory M1"]
        peakA --> valleyB["Basin 2 - Stored Memory M2"]
        peakA --> valleyC["Basin 3 - Spurious State S"]
        peakA --> valleyD["Basin 4 - Stored Memory M3"]
        peakB["Another Hill E_mid"] --> valleyB
        peakB --> valleyC
    end
    noteA["Marble rolls downhill until it reaches a basin floor = network converged to a memory"]
    valleyA -.-> noteA
    valleyB -.-> noteA
    valleyC -.-> noteA
    valleyD -.-> noteA
```

## 4.3 Storage and Recall Process — Top-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph storagePhase["Storage Phase One Shot Learning"]
        P1["Pattern 1 Xi_1"] --> Hebb["Hebbian Outer Product W = 1 by N Sum Xi Xi Transpose"]
        P2["Pattern 2 Xi_2"] --> Hebb
        Pp["Pattern p Xi_p"] --> Hebb
        Hebb --> WM["Weight Matrix W symmetric w_ii = 0"]
    end
    subgraph recallPhase["Recall Phase Iterative Dynamics"]
        Cue["Noisy or Partial Input S 0"] --> Init["Initialize Network State"]
        Init --> Loop["Loop until convergence"]
        Loop --> Update["Asynchronous Update s_i = sgn Sum w_ij s_j"]
        Update --> Converge{"Fixed point reached ?"}
        Converge -->|No| Loop
        Converge -->|Yes| Output["Recalled Memory S_final = argmin E S"]
    end
    WM --> Loop
```

# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** **Q1. Define a Hopfield neural network. Mention its two main operational phases.**

**Model Answer:**
A Hopfield network is a single-layer, fully recurrent neural network with **symmetric weights** ($w_{ij} = w_{ji}$), **no self-connections** ($w_{ii} = 0$), and **bipolar binary neurons** $s_i \in \{-1, +1\}$. It functions as **content-addressable associative memory**.

The two operational phases are:
1. **Storage Phase** — Patterns are stored by setting weights using the **outer-product (Hebbian) rule**:
$$w_{ij} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{\mu=1}^{p} \xi_i^{\mu} \xi_j^{\mu}, \quad i \neq j$$
2. **Recall Phase** — Given a noisy/partial cue, neuron states are updated asynchronously as $s_i = \text{sgn}\!\left(\sum_j w_{ij} s_j\right)$ until the network converges to a **stable fixed point** that corresponds to the nearest stored memory.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`** **Q2. State and explain the Hopfield energy function. Why is it important?**

**Model Answer:**
The Hopfield energy (Lyapunov) function is:
$$E = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} w_{ij} s_i s_j$$
**Importance:**
- It serves as a **monotonically decreasing scalar potential** under the asynchronous update rule: $\Delta E = -2 s_k h_k \leq 0$.
- Because $E$ is bounded below and the state space is finite ($2^N$ states), the network is **guaranteed to converge** in finite time — a key property for associative memory.
- The **stable fixed points** of the dynamics correspond to **local minima** of $E$, with stored memories being the deepest minima.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3 Internal Choice]`**

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of a discrete Hopfield network**. State the constraints on the weight matrix. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **energy function** of the Hopfield network. Show that the energy is monotonically decreasing under the asynchronous update rule. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Architecture & Weight Constraints

**Architecture:**
A Discrete Hopfield Network consists of $N$ neurons arranged in a **single layer**, with every neuron connected to every other neuron via a bidirectional weighted link. The output of each neuron is fed back as input to all other neurons (hence **recurrent**).

**Diagram (textual):**

```
   s1 ---- w12 ---- s2
    \              /
     \            /
      w13      w23
       \        /
        \      /
         s3 ---
        (and so on for N neurons)
```

**Constraints on Weight Matrix $W$:**
1. **Symmetry:** $w_{ij} = w_{ji}$ for all $i, j$.
2. **No self-connections:** $w_{ii} = 0$ for all $i$.
3. **Finite, real-valued weights:** $w_{ij} \in \mathbb{R}$.

**State update rule:**
$$s_i(t+1) = \text{sgn}\!\left( \sum_{j=1}^{N} w_{ij} s_j(t) \right) = \begin{cases} +1, & h_i \geq 0 \\ -1, & h_i < 0 \end{cases}$$

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Energy Function Derivation

Define the energy function as:
$$E = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} w_{ij} s_i s_j$$
The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ corrects the double-counting due to symmetry, and the minus sign ensures that aligned spins give lower energy.

Now consider an asynchronous update where neuron $k$ flips: $s_k \to s_k' = -s_k$. The energy change is:
$$\Delta E = E_{\text{new}} - E_{\text{old}} = (E_{\text{new}}^{(k)} - E_{\text{old}}^{(k)}) + (E_{\text{new}}^{(\bar{k})} - E_{\text{old}}^{(\bar{k})})$$
Since only neuron $k$ changes, the second bracket vanishes. Extracting the $k$-dependent terms:
$$E^{(k)} = -s_k \sum_{j \neq k} w_{kj} s_j = -s_k h_k$$
Therefore:
$$\Delta E = (-s_k' h_k) - (-s_k h_k) = s_k h_k - s_k' h_k = h_k(s_k - s_k') = 2 s_k h_k - 2(s_k)^2 = -2 s_k h_k$$
(using $s_k' = -s_k$). Since the update rule sets $s_k' = \text{sgn}(h_k)$, the product $s_k h_k$ is always non-negative:
$$s_k h_k = \vert h_k \vert \geq 0 \implies \Delta E = -2 \vert h_k \vert \leq 0$$
Hence, $E$ is **monotonically non-increasing**, and since $E$ is bounded below and takes discrete values, the network **converges in finite time** to a stable fixed point. $\blacksquare$

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Hebbian learning rule** for storing patterns in a Hopfield network. How is the weight matrix computed for $p$ patterns? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **storage capacity** of a Hopfield network. What are **spurious states**? How can they be reduced? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Hebbian Learning Rule

The Hopfield network uses **one-shot Hebbian (outer-product) learning** — no iterative training is required. For a single pattern $\boldsymbol{\xi}^{\mu} = (\xi_1^{\mu}, \ldots, \xi_N^{\mu})^T$, the weight that makes $\xi_i^{\mu}$ and $\xi_j^{\mu}$ simultaneously active is:
$$w_{ij} \propto \xi_i^{\mu} \xi_j^{\mu}$$
For $p$ patterns $\boldsymbol{\xi}^{1}, \boldsymbol{\xi}^{2}, \ldots, \boldsymbol{\xi}^{p}$, the rule generalizes via superposition:
$$w_{ij} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{\mu=1}^{p} \xi_i^{\mu} \xi_j^{\mu}, \quad i \neq j$$
In matrix form:
$$W = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{\mu=1}^{p} \boldsymbol{\xi}^{\mu} (\boldsymbol{\xi}^{\mu})^T - \frac{p}{N} I$$
The $-\frac{p}{N} I$ term enforces $w_{ii} = 0$ since $(\xi_i^{\mu})^2 = 1$ for bipolar patterns, so the diagonal would otherwise be $\frac{p}{N}$.

**Verification:** Plugging back the pattern $\xi^{\nu}$ into the local field gives:
$$h_i^{\nu} = \sum_{j \neq i} w_{ij} \xi_j^{\nu} = \xi_i^{\nu}\left(1 - \frac{1}{N}\right) + \underbrace{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{\mu \neq \nu}\xi_i^{\mu} \sum_{j \neq i} \xi_j^{\mu} \xi_j^{\nu}}_{\text{crosstalk noise } C_i^{\nu}}$$
For small $p$, the signal dominates and the pattern is a fixed point.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Storage Capacity & Spurious States

**Storage Capacity:**
- **Heuristic bound**: $p \leq N$ (from $\vert \text{signal}\vert > \vert \text{crosstalk}\vert$).
- **Rigorous bound (Amit–Gutfreund–Sompolinsky, 1987)** using statistical mechanics of disordered systems:
$$p_{\max} \approx 0.138 \, N$$
- For $N = 100$ neurons, only about **13–14 patterns** can be reliably stored.

**Spurious States:**
Spurious states are **stable fixed points of the dynamics that do not correspond to any stored memory**. They arise from:
1. **Mixture states** — Linear combinations of an odd number of stored patterns: e.g., $\text{sgn}(\xi^{\mu} + \xi^{\nu} + \xi^{\sigma})$.
2. **Reverse states** — The negation $-\xi^{\mu}$ of a stored pattern.
3. **Spin-glass states** — Random, uncorrelated minima dominating when $p \gg p_{\max}$.

**Methods to Reduce Spurious States:**
- **Unlearning (Hopfield–Feinstein, 1986)**: Run the network with random initial states, collect the attractors, and *subtract* their contributions from $W$:
$$\Delta w_{ij} = -\epsilon \, s_i^{\text{spurious}} s_j^{\text{spurious}}$$
- **Use orthogonal patterns** to minimize crosstalk.
- **Pseudo-inverse (Kanter–Stiegler) rule**:
$$W = \frac{1}{N} \boldsymbol{\Xi} \boldsymbol{\Xi}^{+} - \frac{p}{N} I$$
where $\boldsymbol{\Xi}^{+}$ is the Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse. This raises capacity to $p \sim N$.
- **Limit pattern count** to $p < 0.138\,N$.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Hopfield Network Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/N$ factor** in $w_{ij}$ — examiners *will* deduct 1 mark. Always normalize.
> 2. **Forgetting $w_{ii} = 0$** — explicitly subtract $\frac{p}{N}I$ when writing the matrix form.
> 3. **Confusing "convergence" with "correct recall"** — convergence only guarantees a *fixed point*, not necessarily the *intended* memory (spurious states!).
> 4. **Forgetting to state the symmetry constraint** when asked about energy — a *2-mark* common question; failure to mention $w_{ij} = w_{ji}$ loses the mark.
> 5. **Storing patterns without verifying the stability condition** $\text{sgn}(h_i^{\mu}) = \xi_i^{\mu}$ — always plug back the pattern to confirm it is a fixed point.
> 6. **Mixing up "stability" with "storing"** — a network can store a pattern in $W$ without it being stable if $p$ is too large.
> 7. **Quoting $p_{\max} = N$ alone** — the **Amit–Gutfreund–Sompolinsky bound $0.138\,N$** is the rigorous answer examiners expect.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Hopfield (1982) network = **recurrent, symmetric, no self-loops, bipolar**.
- ✅ Two phases: **Storage (Hebbian, one-shot)** and **Recall (asynchronous iterative update)**.
- ✅ Update rule: $s_i \leftarrow \text{sgn}\!\left(\sum_j w_{ij} s_j\right)$.
- ✅ Hebbian weight formula: $w_{ij} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{\mu=1}^{p} \xi_i^{\mu} \xi_j^{\mu},\; i \neq j$.
- ✅ Energy: $E = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i}\sum_{j} w_{ij} s_i s_j$ — always non-increasing.
- ✅ Energy change on flip of neuron $k$: $\Delta E_k = -2 s_k h_k \leq 0$.
- ✅ Convergence is **guaranteed** because $E$ is bounded and the state space ($2^N$ states) is finite.
- ✅ **Capacity bound**: $p_{\max} \approx 0.138\,N$ (Amit–Gutfreund–Sompolinsky).
- ✅ **Spurious states** = unwanted fixed points (mixtures, reverses, spin-glass).
- ✅ **Pseudo-inverse rule** and **unlearning** improve capacity and reduce spurious states.
- ✅ **Continuous Hopfield Network (1984)**: uses $\tanh$ activation and ODE dynamics; useful for optimization (TSP, ADC).
- ✅ Tank–Hopfield (1986) used CHN for **analog-to-digital conversion** — a classic KTU essay point.
- ✅ Symmetry ($w_{ij} = w_{ji}$) + zero diagonal ($w_{ii} = 0$) are the **two essential preconditions** for the existence of a Lyapunov energy function.
- ✅ For a noisy input, the network performs **pattern completion** by relaxing to the nearest basin of attraction in the energy landscape.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Support Vector Machines (SVM)

A **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a supervised discriminative binary classifier formally defined as a maximum-margin hyperplane-based decision function that, given a labeled training dataset, learns an optimal separating hyperplane by solving a convex quadratic optimization problem whose objective is to maximize the geometric distance (margin) between the decision boundary and the nearest data points of opposing classes. The points that lie closest to the decision boundary are called **support vectors**, and they alone determine the position and orientation of the optimal hyperplane.

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** Within Module 3, students are expected to formally distinguish between **Hard-Margin SVM** (strict linear separability assumption, no misclassification) and **Soft-Margin SVM** (introduces slack variables $\xi_i$ to tolerate misclassifications and overlapping classes).

### 1.2 Hard-Margin vs. Soft-Margin SVM — The Core Distinction

| Attribute | Hard-Margin SVM | Soft-Margin SVM |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Assumption** | Strictly linearly separable | Allows overlapping / non-separable data |
| **Misclassifications** | Forbidden | Permitted, penalized via slack variables |
| **Slack Variables $\xi_i$** | Not used | Introduced ($\xi_i \geq 0$) |
| **Regularization Parameter $C$** | Not required | Required (controls trade-off) |
| **Optimality** | Unique, deterministic | Trade-off between margin width and error |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **🛣️ The "Highway Between Two Villages" Analogy**
> 
> Imagine two rival villages (Class $+1$ and Class $-1$) sitting on opposite sides of a long, flat plain. You, as a town planner, must draw a **road (the hyperplane)** between them. The rule is simple: **the road must be as wide as possible** to keep the maximum number of villagers safe from cross-village conflicts.
> 
> - The **widest possible road** is the *maximum margin*.
> - The **houses closest to the road** (on either side) are the *support vectors*. They, and only they, dictate where the road is built.
> - If a few rogue villagers (outliers) build houses in the middle of the road's intended path, you have two choices:
>   - **Hard-Margin SVM:** Knock down those houses (works only if they can be cleanly removed, i.e., the villages are perfectly separable).
>   - **Soft-Margin SVM:** Slightly bend the rules and allow the road to be narrower at that point, paying a "fine" (the penalty term $C \cdot \xi_i$) for the inconvenience. This is the realistic engineering solution.

### 1.4 Why SVMs Matter in KTU/Engineering Practice

- **Robustness:** Maximizing the margin provides strong **generalization** guarantees (bound by VC dimension theory).
- **Versatility:** Through the **kernel trick**, SVMs can solve non-linear problems without ever computing coordinates in the higher-dimensional space.
- **Applications:** Bio-informatics (cancer classification), image recognition (face detection via HOG + linear SVM), text categorization (spam filtering), handwriting recognition (MNIST), and time-series forecasting in power systems.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Geometric Foundation — Hyperplane, Margin, and Support Vectors

Given a binary classification dataset:
$$\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}, \quad \mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d, \quad y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$$

A **separating hyperplane** is the set of points satisfying:
$$\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$$

where $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the **weight (normal) vector** and $b \in \mathrm{R}$ is the **bias term**.

**Geometric (Perpendicular) Distance from a Point to the Hyperplane:**
$$\text{distance}(\mathbf{x}_i, H) = \frac{\vert \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2}$$

**Functional Margin** (signed, scale-dependent):
$$\hat{\gamma}_i = y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b)$$

**Geometric Margin** (true perpendicular distance, scale-invariant):
$$\gamma_i = \frac{y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2}$$

The minimum geometric margin over the training set is:
$$\gamma = \min_{i} \gamma_i = \min_i \frac{y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2}$$

### 2.2 Hard-Margin SVM — The Primal Formulation

**Logic Steps (Operational Derivation Logic):**

1. **Normalize the scale:** Impose the canonical constraint $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ for all $i$. This forces every point to lie on or beyond the margin, and crucially, makes the *functional* margin equal to **1** for the support vectors.
2. **Compute the margin width:** Under this canonical form, the two canonical margin hyperplanes $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = +1$ and $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = -1$ are separated by a perpendicular distance of $\frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$.
3. **Maximize the margin:** $\max \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ is mathematically equivalent to $\min \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$.
4. **Convexity:** The objective is a convex quadratic, the constraints are linear, $\Rightarrow$ **a unique global optimum** is guaranteed.

**Hard-Margin Primal Optimization Problem:**
$$\boxed{\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \; \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \;\; \forall i = 1, \dots, N}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is included purely for mathematical convenience — it cancels the factor of 2 that appears when differentiating $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ via calculus.

### 2.3 Hard-Margin SVM — The Dual Formulation

Using **Lagrange multipliers** $\alpha_i \geq 0$, the Lagrangian is:
$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \right]$$

Setting partial derivatives to zero:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$$
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

Substituting back yields the **Hard-Margin Dual Problem**:
$$\boxed{\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \alpha_i \geq 0, \;\; \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0}$$

**KKT Complementary Slackness Conditions:**
$$\alpha_i \left[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \right] = 0, \quad \forall i$$

- $\alpha_i = 0$: point is *not* a support vector (lies strictly outside the margin).
- $\alpha_i > 0$: point **is** a support vector, lies exactly on the margin.

### 2.4 Soft-Margin SVM — The Primal Formulation

**Why Soft-Margin?** Real engineering data is rarely perfectly separable. Allowing some violations is the *only* way to achieve a useful classifier.

**Step 1: Introduce slack variables** $\xi_i \geq 0$ to measure the *degree of violation* of the $i$-th point:
$$y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$$

**Step 2: Add a penalty** proportional to the total slack:
$$\boxed{\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \; \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \;\; \xi_i \geq 0, \;\; \forall i}$$

- $\xi_i = 0$: Point is correctly classified and outside or on the margin.
- $0 < \xi_i \leq 1$: Point is correctly classified but **inside the margin**.
- $\xi_i > 1$: Point is **misclassified**.

**Step 3: The regularization parameter $C > 0$:**
- **Large $C$** $\Rightarrow$ Heavy penalty for violations $\Rightarrow$ Narrow margin, fewer errors (risk of overfitting).
- **Small $C$** $\Rightarrow$ Light penalty $\Rightarrow$ Wider margin, more violations tolerated (risk of underfitting).

### 2.5 Soft-Margin SVM — The Dual Formulation

The Soft-Margin dual differs from Hard-Margin *only* in the upper bound on the multipliers:
$$\boxed{\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j) \quad \text{subject to} \quad 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C, \;\; \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0}$$

**Interpretation of $\alpha_i$ in Soft-Margin SVM:**
- $\alpha_i = 0$: Not a support vector.
- $0 < \alpha_i < C$: Support vector **on the margin** ($\xi_i = 0$).
- $\alpha_i = C$: Support vector **violating the margin** ($\xi_i > 0$).

### 2.6 The Hinge Loss Interpretation

The Soft-Margin objective is equivalent to **Empirical Risk Minimization** with the **Hinge Loss**:
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{hinge}}(\mathbf{w}, b; \mathbf{x}_i, y_i) = \max(0, \, 1 - y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b))$$

$$J(\mathbf{w}, b) = \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \max(0, 1 - y_i f(\mathbf{x}_i))$$

The hinge loss is **convex**, **non-differentiable at $y_i f(\mathbf{x}_i) = 1$**, and **zero** when the point is correctly classified beyond the margin.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b$ | Decision function (hyperplane) |
| 2 | $\text{margin} = \frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Perpendicular distance between canonical margins |
| 3 | $\min \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ | Hard-Margin primal objective |
| 4 | $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ | Soft-Margin margin constraint |
| 5 | $\min \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum \xi_i$ | Soft-Margin primal objective |
| 6 | $\sum \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum \sum \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | Dual objective (with kernel $K$) |
| 7 | $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ | Soft-Margin box constraint |
| 8 | $\mathbf{w} = \sum \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ | Weight expressed via support vectors |
| 9 | $L_{\text{hinge}} = \max(0, 1 - y f(\mathbf{x}))$ | Hinge loss function |
| 10 | $\gamma_{\min} = \min_i \frac{y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Minimum geometric margin |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Classifying malignant vs. benign tumors from gene-expression data.
- **Power Systems:** Fault detection in transmission lines using time-series features.
- **Structural Health Monitoring:** Classifying vibration signatures for damage detection.
- **NLP:** Text categorization where high-dimensional sparse vectors are standard.
- **Signal Processing:** Modulation classification in cognitive radio.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Derivation: From Hard-Margin Primal to Dual

**Step 1 — Start with the Hard-Margin Primal Problem.**

The goal is to find the hyperplane that maximizes the geometric margin. Geometrically, the margin is the perpendicular distance between the two closest points (one from each class) and the hyperplane. If the closest points are at signed distances $+d$ and $-d$, the total margin is $2d$.

The geometric margin of the $i$-th point is:
$$\gamma_i = \frac{y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2}$$

Maximizing the minimum margin:
$$\max_{\mathbf{w}, b} \; \gamma = \min_{i} \frac{y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Canonical Rescaling Trick.**

We are free to rescale $(\mathbf{w}, b)$ without changing the hyperplane (since $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ is scale-invariant). Impose the convention that for the *nearest* point:
$$y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$$

Under this convention, the minimum geometric margin becomes:
$$\gamma = \min_i \frac{y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2} = \frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2}$$

So the optimization problem becomes:
$$\max_{\mathbf{w}, b} \; \frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2} \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \;\; \forall i$$

**Step 3 — Convert Maximization to Minimization (Mathematical Convenience).**

Since maximizing $\frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2}$ is equivalent to minimizing $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2$, and squaring preserves the monotonicity, we obtain:
$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \; \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2^2 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$$

A scalar factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ is introduced (this will cancel the factor of 2 upon differentiation):
$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \; \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2^2 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$$

This is the **Hard-Margin Primal**.

**Step 4 — Form the Lagrangian.**

Introduce one Lagrange multiplier $\alpha_i \geq 0$ per inequality constraint:
$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \right]$$

The negative sign in front of the summation is because the inequality is "$\geq$" (minimization uses $+$ for $\geq$ constraints when subtracting).

**Step 5 — Compute Stationarity Conditions.**

Differentiate with respect to $\mathbf{w}$:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w} - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i = 0$$
$$\Rightarrow \mathbf{w}^\star = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$$

Differentiate with respect to $b$:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$
$$\Rightarrow \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Back into the Lagrangian.**

Substitute $\mathbf{w} = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ into $\frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$:
$$\frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{w} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j)$$

Substitute into the Lagrangian and use the constraint $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$:
$$\mathcal{L}_D(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j) - \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i - b \sum_i \alpha_i y_i + \sum_i \alpha_i$$

The middle terms vanish due to the stationarity conditions:
$$\mathcal{L}_D(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j)$$

**Step 7 — State the Dual Problem.**

$$\boxed{\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j)}$$
$$\text{subject to} \quad \alpha_i \geq 0, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

---

### 3.2 Complete Derivation: Soft-Margin Primal to Dual

**Step 1 — Primal Form.**

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \; \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$$
$$\text{s.t.} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0, \;\; \forall i$$

**Step 2 — Form the Lagrangian.**

Introduce multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ (for the margin constraint) and $\mu_i \geq 0$ (for the $\xi_i \geq 0$ constraint):
$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_i \xi_i - \sum_i \alpha_i \left[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i \right] - \sum_i \mu_i \xi_i$$

**Step 3 — Stationarity with respect to $\xi_i$.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} = C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \mu_i = C - \alpha_i$$

Since $\mu_i \geq 0$, we get the **box constraint**:
$$\alpha_i \leq C$$

**Step 4 — Stationarity with respect to $\mathbf{w}$ and $b$ (same as Hard-Margin):**
$$\mathbf{w} = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i, \quad \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Back to Obtain the Dual.**

The dual objective is *identical* to Hard-Margin, but the feasible region is now bounded:
$$\boxed{\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \; \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j)}$$
$$\text{s.t.} \quad 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C, \quad \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Hard-Margin SVM

> **Dataset:** 2-D points: $A = (1, 1), y_A = +1$; $B = (2, 2), y_B = +1$; $C = (0, 0), y_C = -1$; $D = (1, 0), y_D = -1$.

**Goal:** Find the maximum-margin separating hyperplane.

**Step 1 — Identify Support Vectors by Inspection.**

Notice that the convex hulls touch at the line $x_2 = x_1$. The points closest to the opposing class are $A = (1,1)$ from $+1$ class and $D = (1,0)$ from $-1$ class (and $C = (0,0)$). The optimal hyperplane lies equidistant from $A$ and $D$.

**Step 2 — Solve Primal Directly.**

For $A$: $\mathbf{w}^\top (1,1) + b = 1$
For $D$: $\mathbf{w}^\top (1,0) + b = -1$

Subtracting: $w_2 = 2 \Rightarrow w_1 + 2 + b = 1 \Rightarrow w_1 + b = -1$.

Solving the second: $w_1 + b = -1$, so $b = -1 - w_1$. From first: $w_1 + 1 \cdot 1 + w_1 + b = w_1 + 1 + w_1 + b = 2w_1 + 1 + b = 1 \Rightarrow 2w_1 + b = 0 \Rightarrow 2w_1 - 1 - w_1 = 0 \Rightarrow w_1 = 1$, $b = -2$.

**Step 3 — Final Solution.**
$$\mathbf{w} = (1, 2), \quad b = -2, \quad \text{Hyperplane: } x_1 + 2x_2 - 2 = 0$$

Verify with $B = (2,2)$: $2 + 4 - 2 = 4 \geq 1$ ✓ (correctly classified, outside margin).
Verify with $C = (0,0)$: $0 + 0 - 2 = -2 \leq -1$ ✓ (correctly classified, outside margin).

**Margin width:** $\frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{1^2 + 2^2}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 0.894$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Soft-Margin SVM (with Slack)

> **Dataset:** $A = (1, 1), y_A = +1$; $B = (2, 0.5), y_B = +1$; $C = (1.5, 2), y_C = -1$ (an outlier inside the $+1$ cluster).

A hard-margin SVM cannot perfectly separate these. With $C = 1$, the soft-margin formulation will tolerate the violation.

**Step 1 — Solve the dual numerically** (or via a QP solver) to obtain $\alpha_i$ values. Suppose the solution yields:
$$\alpha_A = 0.4, \quad \alpha_B = 0.6, \quad \alpha_C = 1.0 \;(\text{at the upper bound } C)$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\mathbf{w}$.**
$$\mathbf{w} = \alpha_A y_A \mathbf{x}_A + \alpha_B y_B \mathbf{x}_B + \alpha_C y_C \mathbf{x}_C$$
$$= 0.4(1)(1,1) + 0.6(1)(2, 0.5) + 1.0(-1)(1.5, 2)$$
$$= (0.4, 0.4) + (1.2, 0.3) + (-1.5, -2) = (0.1, -1.3)$$

**Step 3 — Compute $b$ from a margin-supporting vector** (any $i$ with $0 < \alpha_i < C$; suppose $A$ qualifies):
$$b = y_A - \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_A = 1 - (0.1 \cdot 1 + (-1.3) \cdot 1) = 1 - (-1.2) = 2.2$$

**Step 4 — Final decision function:**
$$f(\mathbf{x}) = 0.1 x_1 - 1.3 x_2 + 2.2$$

**Step 5 — Check the outlier** $C = (1.5, 2)$:
$$f(C) = 0.15 - 2.6 + 2.2 = -0.25$$

Since $f(C) < 0$, it is misclassified (it should be $-1$, which is consistent), but it is assigned a non-zero slack $\xi_C > 0$, and $\alpha_C = C$ saturates the box constraint — exactly the signature of a soft-margin violation.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Hard-Margin SVM | Soft-Margin SVM |
|---|---|---|
| **Primal Objective** | $\frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ | $\frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C\sum \xi_i$ |
| **Margin Constraint** | $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ | $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ |
| **Slack Constraint** | None | $\xi_i \geq 0$ |
| **Dual Constraint on $\alpha_i$** | $\alpha_i \geq 0$ | $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ |
| **Dual Objective** | Same form | Same form |
| **KKT Status** | Complementary slackness | Box constraints + slackness |
| **Convexity** | Strictly convex QP | Strictly convex QP |
| **Loss Function** | Cannot be misclassified | Hinge loss: $\max(0, 1 - y f(\mathbf{x}))$ |
| **When to Use** | Clean, noise-free data | Real-world, noisy data |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Hard-Margin SVM Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Training Data XN labeled Y in plus1 minus1] --> B[Scale Canonicalization w x plus b equals 1]
    B --> C[Primal QP Formulation min 0.5 w squared]
    C --> D{Lagrange Multipliers alpha i ge 0}
    D --> E[Stationarity w equals sum alpha i yi xi]
    D --> F[Stationarity sum alpha i yi equals 0]
    E --> G[Dual QP Maximization]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Identify Support Vectors alpha i greater than 0]
    H --> I[Recover w and b]
    I --> J[Decision Function f of x equals w trans x plus b]
    J --> K[Predict sign of f of x]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Soft-Margin SVM Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Training Data with overlap] --> B[Introduce Slack Variables xi i ge 0]
    B --> C[Primal Formulation min 0.5 w squared plus C sum xi]
    C --> D[Two Multipliers alpha i and mu i]
    D --> E[Stationarity w equals sum alpha i yi xi]
    D --> F[Stationarity sum alpha i yi equals 0]
    D --> G[Stationarity C minus alpha i minus mu i equals 0]
    E --> H[Dual QP with Box Constraint]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Classify Support Vectors into three cases]
    I --> J1[alpha i equals 0 not SV]
    I --> J2[0 less alpha i less C on margin]
    I --> J3[alpha i equals C violating margin]
    J1 --> K[Recover w and b]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    K --> L[Decision Function and Hinge Loss Evaluation]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Geometry — Margin, Support Vectors, and Slacks

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POS[Class plus 1 Region]
        P1[Point P1 inside margin xi between 0 and 1]
        P2[Point P2 on margin support vector]
        P3[Point P3 far from margin regular point]
    end
    subgraph NEG[Class minus 1 Region]
        N1[Point N1 misclassified xi greater than 1]
        N2[Point N2 on margin support vector]
        N3[Point N3 far from margin regular point]
    end
    H[Optimal Hyperplane H: w trans x plus b equals 0]
    HMP[Plus Margin Boundary: w trans x plus b equals 1]
    HMN[Minus Margin Boundary: w trans x plus b equals minus 1]
    HMP --- H
    H --- HMN
    POS --- HMP
    HMN --- NEG
    P2 -. on margin .-> HMP
    P1 -. violates .-> HMP
    N2 -. on margin .-> HMN
    N1 -. violates .-> HMN
```

### 4.4 Process Flow — Training to Prediction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start] --> S2[Collect and Preprocess Data]
    S2 --> S3{Linearly Separable?}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[Use Hard-Margin SVM]
    S3 -- No --> S5[Choose Regularization C]
    S5 --> S6[Use Soft-Margin SVM]
    S4 --> S7[Solve Primal or Dual QP]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Identify Support Vectors]
    S8 --> S9[Compute Hyperplane w and b]
    S9 --> S10[Deploy Classifier]
    S10 --> S11{Predict Label}
    S11 -- f x greater than 0 --> S12[Label equals plus 1]
    S11 -- f x less than 0 --> S13[Label equals minus 1]
    S12 --> S14[End]
    S13 --> S14
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1. Define the concept of "support vectors" in an SVM. Why are they crucial to the model's solution?**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> Support vectors are the training samples that lie *exactly on* the margin hyperplanes (i.e., $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$) or violate them ($\xi_i > 0$ in the soft-margin case). They correspond to the data points with non-zero Lagrange multipliers ($\alpha_i > 0$). They are crucial because, by the representer theorem, the weight vector is expressed as $\mathbf{w} = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$; hence only the support vectors contribute to $\mathbf{w}$. The entire decision boundary is *determined* by these few points, making SVMs memory-efficient and robust to changes in non-support points.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between Hard-Margin and Soft-Margin SVMs. When is the latter preferred?**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> | Aspect | Hard-Margin SVM | Soft-Margin SVM |
> |---|---|---|
> | Data Assumption | Strictly linearly separable | Allows overlapping/non-separable data |
> | Slack Variables | None | $\xi_i \geq 0$ introduced |
> | Penalty Parameter | Not required | $C$ controls trade-off |
> | Soft-Margin is preferred when: (1) the dataset is noisy, (2) classes overlap in feature space, (3) perfect linear separation is infeasible or would overfit, and (4) a controlled margin-error trade-off is desired. |

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:**
> A common 3-mark mistake is *equating* the margin with the distance from a single point. The **margin** is the *sum* of the perpendicular distances from the two class-defining hyperplanes to the central decision hyperplane, equal to $\frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ under canonical scaling. Students often write it as $\frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ (missing the factor of 2) and lose a mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 3, 14 Marks]**
> **Question A:**
> **(a)** Formulate the **Hard-Margin SVM** primal optimization problem from first principles, clearly defining every variable, the geometric margin, and the role of the canonical rescaling. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the **Hard-Margin Dual** from the primal using the method of Lagrange multipliers, stating all KKT conditions explicitly. Discuss what the KKT complementary slackness condition reveals about the role of support vectors. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Primal Formulation (7 marks)**

1. **Setup:** Given training set $\{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ and a candidate hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$.
2. **Geometric margin definition:** For point $i$, the signed perpendicular distance is $\gamma_i = \frac{y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$. The overall margin is $\gamma = \min_i \gamma_i$.
3. **Canonical rescaling:** Impose the convention that for the *closest* point, the functional margin equals 1, i.e., $\min_i y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$. Then $\gamma = \frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$.
4. **Maximization converted to minimization:**
$$\max_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} \equiv \min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$$
5. **Primal Problem:**
$$\boxed{\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \;\; \forall i = 1, \dots, N}$$

**Part (b) — Dual Derivation & KKT (7 marks)**

1. **Lagrangian:** Introduce $\alpha_i \geq 0$:
$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 - \sum_i \alpha_i [y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1]$$
2. **Stationarity:**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{w} = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$$
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$$
3. **Substitution back** yields the dual:
$$\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \alpha_i \geq 0,\; \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$$
4. **KKT Conditions:**
   - Primal feasibility: $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$.
   - Dual feasibility: $\alpha_i \geq 0$.
   - Stationarity: as above.
   - **Complementary slackness:** $\alpha_i [y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1] = 0$.
5. **Implication:** If $\alpha_i > 0$, then the bracket must be zero, meaning the point lies *on* the margin — these are the support vectors. If $\alpha_i = 0$, the point is irrelevant to the solution. The decision boundary is *fully determined* by support vectors.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 3, 14 Marks]**
> **Question B:**
> **(a)** Explain why **Hard-Margin SVM** fails on non-separable data. Introduce slack variables $\xi_i$ and formulate the **Soft-Margin SVM** primal problem. Discuss the role of the regularization parameter $C$. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the **Soft-Margin SVM Dual** and the **Hinge Loss** interpretation of the objective. Show how the Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i$ classify the support vectors into three categories. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Soft-Margin Primal (7 marks)**

1. **Failure of Hard-Margin:** The constraint $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ for all $i$ becomes infeasible when classes overlap, so the feasible set is empty — no hyperplane exists.
2. **Slack variables:** Introduce $\xi_i \geq 0$ to allow margin violations:
$$y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0$$
3. **Penalty term:** Add $C \sum_i \xi_i$ to the objective:
$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_i \xi_i$$
4. **Role of $C$:**
   - **Large $C$:** Heavy penalty $\Rightarrow$ narrow margin, fewer violations, lower bias, higher variance.
   - **Small $C$:** Light penalty $\Rightarrow$ wide margin, more violations tolerated, higher bias, lower variance.
5. **Interpretation of $\xi_i$:** $\xi_i = 0$ (correct & outside margin), $0 < \xi_i \leq 1$ (correct but inside margin), $\xi_i > 1$ (misclassified).

**Part (b) — Soft-Margin Dual & Hinge Loss (7 marks)**

1. **Lagrangian with two multipliers** $\alpha_i \geq 0$ (margin) and $\mu_i \geq 0$ (slack):
$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_i \xi_i - \sum_i \alpha_i [y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i] - \sum_i \mu_i \xi_i$$
2. **Stationarity in $\xi_i$:** $C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0$. Since $\mu_i \geq 0$, we get $\alpha_i \leq C$, yielding the **box constraint** $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$.
3. **Soft-Margin Dual** (objective identical to hard-margin):
$$\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C, \; \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$$
4. **Hinge Loss Equivalence:** The soft-margin objective is equivalent to regularized hinge loss:
$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_i \max(0, 1 - y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b))$$
5. **Three categories of support vectors based on $\alpha_i$:**
   - $\alpha_i = 0$: regular point (correctly classified, outside margin).
   - $0 < \alpha_i < C$: on-margin support vector, $\xi_i = 0$.
   - $\alpha_i = C$: margin-violating support vector, $\xi_i > 0$.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #2:**
> **Common 14-mark mistakes students make:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$** in the primal objective. While technically the optimum is unchanged, KTU valuations expect the canonical form $\frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ — losing $\frac{1}{2}$ mark if omitted.
> 2. **Conflating primal and dual constraints.** Hard-Margin dual has $\alpha_i \geq 0$; Soft-Margin dual has $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$. Mixing these up is a frequent 2-mark error.
> 3. **Omitting the KKT stationarity condition** $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$ in the dual — KTU examiners explicitly check for this. It typically costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not writing the KKT complementary slackness condition explicitly.** It is the *interpretive link* between the dual variables and the support vectors. Skipping it loses ~2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing geometric margin ($\frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$) with functional margin.** The functional margin is scale-dependent; the geometric margin is the *true* perpendicular distance.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3: SVMs)**

- [x] **SVM Objective:** Maximize geometric margin $\frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ between two classes.
- [x] **Canonical Form:** Imposing $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ for the closest point normalizes the formulation.
- [x] **Primal — Hard:** $\min \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$ s.t. $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$.
- [x] **Primal — Soft:** $\min \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum \xi_i$ s.t. $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$, $\xi_i \geq 0$.
- [x] **Dual — Hard:** $\max \sum \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum \sum \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ s.t. $\alpha_i \geq 0$, $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$.
- [x] **Dual — Soft:** Same objective, but with **box constraint** $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$.
- [x] **Representer Theorem:** $\mathbf{w} = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ — only support vectors (with $\alpha_i > 0$) contribute to $\mathbf{w}$.
- [x] **Hinge Loss:** $\max(0, 1 - y_i f(\mathbf{x}_i))$ — convex, non-differentiable at the margin, zero beyond it.
- [x] **Role of $C$:** Trades off margin width against misclassification; large $C$ = low bias/high variance; small $C$ = high bias/low variance.
- [x] **Three $\alpha_i$ Cases (Soft-Margin):** $0$ (regular), $\in (0, C)$ (on-margin SV), $= C$ (violating SV).
- [x] **KKT Complementary Slackness:** $\alpha_i [y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1] = 0$.
- [x] **Why Dual?** Enables the **kernel trick** by replacing $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ with $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ for non-linear classification.
- [x] **Engineering Applications:** Bio-informatics, image recognition, text classification, fault diagnosis, and time-series analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concepts of Kernels and Feature Spaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT525 - ARTIFICIAL NEURAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concepts of Kernels and Feature Spaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Feature Space and Kernels

In the formal setting of **Pattern Recognition** and **Statistical Learning Theory**, the raw data points (such as pixels, voltage readings, or biological measurements) reside in a low-dimensional space known as the **Input Space** $\mathcal{X} \subset \mathbb{R}^d$. Many real-world classification problems are **not linearly separable** in this raw input space. To resolve this, the input vectors are mathematically transformed into a new, higher-dimensional vector space called the **Feature Space** $\mathcal{F}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feature Space (KTU 2024 Definition):** A vector space $\mathcal{F}$ into which the original input vectors $\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{X}$ are mapped by a (possibly nonlinear) feature map $\boldsymbol{\phi}: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{F}$, such that the inner product structure in $\mathcal{F}$ enables linear learning algorithms to solve nonlinear problems in $\mathcal{X}$.

Mathematically, the feature map is expressed as:

$$\boldsymbol{\phi}: \mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{d} \longrightarrow \mathcal{F} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{D}$$

where $D \gg d$ in general. The mapped feature vector is:

$$\boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} \phi_{1}(\mathbf{x}) \\ \phi_{2}(\mathbf{x}) \\ \vdots \\ \phi_{D}(\mathbf{x}) \end{bmatrix}$$

### 1.2 The Kernel Function — Rigorous Definition

A **Kernel Function** is a symmetric, real-valued function $K: \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ that computes the inner product of two input vectors after they have been mapped into the feature space:

$$K(\mathbf{x}_{i}, \mathbf{x}_{j}) = \langle \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{i}), \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{j}) \rangle_{\mathcal{F}} = \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{i})^{\top} \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_{j})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Kernel (KTU 2024 Highlight):** A function $K$ is called a valid kernel if it corresponds to an inner product in some (possibly infinite-dimensional) feature space. The defining property is that $K$ can be decomposed as $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_i)^{\top} \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}_j)$ for some mapping $\boldsymbol{\phi}$.

### 1.3 The "Kernel Trick" — Intuitive Analogy

**Imagine a Student Cooking in a Tiny Kitchen (Input Space, 2D):**
You have only two ingredients (sugar, flour) and you cannot make a complex cake. The cakes are hopelessly mixed together. You cannot draw a straight knife line (linear boundary) to cut them apart.

**Now Imagine a Giant Industrial Bakery (Feature Space, 1000D):**
You send each ingredient to the bakery, which automatically adds new "engineered" features (egg content, temperature, humidity, baking time, yeast quantity, etc.). In this richer 1000-dimensional space, you can now easily place a flat sheet (hyperplane) to perfectly separate the cakes!

**The "Trick":** The bakery (kernel) never tells you the exact recipe (feature vector $\boldsymbol{\phi}$). It only tells you the **similarity score** between any two ingredients — which is exactly the inner product in the bakery's world. This saves enormous computation.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this revolutionary?**
> - You **avoid explicitly computing** $\boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x})$ which could be infinite-dimensional.
> - Many learning algorithms (SVM, PCA, Ridge Regression) can be re-written using **only inner products** of training data. Replacing these inner products with $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ yields powerful **nonlinear versions** at **no extra computational cost** — this is the **Kernel Trick**.

### 1.4 Taxonomy of Kernel Methods

| Category | Description | Standard Kernel |
|---|---|---|
| **Stationary Kernels** | Depend only on $\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}'$ | RBF, Laplacian |
| **Non-Stationary Kernels** | Depend on individual locations | Polynomial, Sigmoid |
| **Dot-Product Kernels** | Function of $\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}'$ | Polynomial, Sigmoid |
| **Translation Invariant** | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = K(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}')$ | Gaussian RBF |

### 1.5 KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CO Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **CO3 — Design kernel-based learning models** and **CO4 — Apply Mercer's conditions for kernel validation** in the PEEBT525 syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline of a Kernel Method

The complete operational flow of a kernel-based learner can be decomposed into **four sequential steps**:

1. **Choose the Kernel:** Select a kernel function $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ that encodes the prior belief about the similarity between data points.
2. **Construct the Gram Matrix:** Form the $N \times N$ symmetric matrix $\mathbf{G}$ where $\mathbf{G}_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$.
3. **Validate via Mercer's Condition:** Ensure $\mathbf{G}$ is positive semi-definite (PSD), guaranteeing the existence of a valid feature space.
4. **Solve the Dual Optimization:** Replace every occurrence of $\mathbf{x}_i^{\top} \mathbf{x}_j$ in the primal algorithm with $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ and solve.

### 2.2 Mercer's Theorem — The Theoretical Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Mercer's Theorem (KTU Board-Favorite):** A symmetric function $K: \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is a valid (positive definite) kernel if and only if, for any finite set of input points $\{\mathbf{x}_1, \dots, \mathbf{x}_N\}$, the Gram matrix $\mathbf{G}_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ is **positive semi-definite**, i.e., for any vector $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^N$:

$$\mathbf{c}^{\top} \mathbf{G} \mathbf{c} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} c_i c_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) \geq 0$$

This is the **necessary and sufficient condition** for a kernel to correspond to an inner product in some Hilbert space.

### 2.3 Construction Methods for Valid Kernels

**Closure Properties** — If $K_1$ and $K_2$ are valid kernels, then the following are also valid kernels:

- **Sum:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = K_1(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') + K_2(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}')$
- **Scaling:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \alpha K_1(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}')$ for $\alpha > 0$
- **Product:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = K_1(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') \cdot K_2(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}')$
- **Normalization:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \dfrac{K_1(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}')}{\sqrt{K_1(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}) \cdot K_1(\mathbf{x}', \mathbf{x}')}}$
- **Exponentiation:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \exp(K_1(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}'))$ (only for properly bounded $K_1$)

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Kernel Name** | **Mathematical Form $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}')$** | **Feature Map $\boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x})$ (2D case)** | **Key Parameters** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | $\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}'$ | $\boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}$ | None |
| **Polynomial (Homogeneous)** | $(\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}')^{p}$ | All monomials of total degree $p$ | Degree $p \in \mathbb{N}$ |
| **Polynomial (Inhomogeneous)** | $(\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}' + c)^{p}$ | Monomials up to degree $p$ | Degree $p$, constant $c \geq 0$ |
| **Gaussian RBF** | $\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\vert\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}'\vert\vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$ | Infinite-dimensional | Bandwidth $\sigma > 0$ |
| **Laplacian RBF** | $\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\vert\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}'\vert\vert_{1}}{\gamma}\right)$ | Infinite-dimensional | Width $\gamma > 0$ |
| **Sigmoid (Tanh)** | $\tanh(\alpha \mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}' + c)$ | Not strictly positive definite | Scale $\alpha$, bias $c$ |
| **Cosine Similarity** | $\dfrac{\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}'}{\vert\vert \mathbf{x}\vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert \mathbf{x}'\vert\vert}$ | Normalized features | None |
| **ANOVA Kernel** | $\left(\sum_{k=1}^{d} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x_{k} - x'_{k})^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)\right)^{p}$ | Sparse higher-order interactions | Order $p$, width $\sigma$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions & Validity Rules:**
> - RBF kernel is **always PSD** for any $\sigma > 0$.
> - Polynomial kernel is PSD only for $c \geq 0$.
> - Sigmoid kernel is **NOT a valid Mercer kernel** for arbitrary $\alpha, c$ — it may not yield a PSD Gram matrix.
> - Linear kernel is a **special case** of polynomial with $p=1, c=0$.

### 2.5 The Dual Representation — Why Kernels Work in Algorithms

Consider a regularized risk minimization problem whose optimal weights can be expressed as a linear combination of training inputs:

$$\mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{i} \mathbf{x}_{i}$$

For a new test point $\mathbf{z}$, the prediction becomes:

$$f(\mathbf{z}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{z} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{i} \langle \mathbf{x}_{i}, \mathbf{z} \rangle = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{i} (\mathbf{x}_{i}^{\top} \mathbf{z})$$

The **kernel trick** replaces the inner product with $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{z})$, yielding:

$$f(\mathbf{z}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{i} K(\mathbf{x}_{i}, \mathbf{z})$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Bioinformatics:** Protein classification using spectrum kernels on string sequences.
> - **Computer Vision:** Image classification with RBF kernel SVMs.
> - **Time Series:** ECG/EEG anomaly detection via dynamic time warping kernels.
> - **Communication:** Channel equalization in non-linear digital channels.

### 2.6 Why "Lift-and-Project" Solves Nonlinearity

A classical result: the circle equation $x_1^2 + x_2^2 = r^2$ (a nonlinear curve) becomes the **linear** equation $Z = r^2$ in the lifted space where $Z = x_1^2 + x_2^2$. This is the geometric essence of feature mapping.

| Input Space ($\mathbb{R}^2$) | Feature Space ($\mathbb{R}^3$) | Type of Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| Straight Line | Plane | Linear |
| Circle / Ellipse | Plane | Linear in $x_1^2, x_2^2$ |
| Any polynomial of degree $p$ | Hyperplane | Linear in monomials |
| Arbitrary smooth boundary | Infinite-dim hyperplane | Always linear in $\mathcal{F}$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Polynomial Kernel from Explicit Feature Map (2D Case)

**Objective:** Show that $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = (\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}' + c)^{2}$ admits a closed-form feature map $\boldsymbol{\phi}$.

Let $\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x_{1} \\ x_{2} \end{bmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{x}' = \begin{bmatrix} x'_{1} \\ x'_{2} \end{bmatrix}$. Take $c \in \mathbb{R}$, and set $p = 2$.

**Step 1:** Expand the kernel expression using the binomial theorem.

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = (\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}' + c)^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Apply $(a+b)^{2} = a^{2} + 2ab + b^{2}$ with $a = \mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}'$ and $b = c$.

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = (\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}')^{2} + 2c(\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}') + c^{2}$$

**Step 3:** Expand the inner product $\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}' = x_{1}x'_{1} + x_{2}x'_{2}$.

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = (x_{1}x'_{1} + x_{2}x'_{2})^{2} + 2c(x_{1}x'_{1} + x_{2}x'_{2}) + c^{2}$$

**Step 4:** Square the binomial term to obtain monomials.

$$(x_{1}x'_{1} + x_{2}x'_{2})^{2} = x_{1}^{2}x'^{2}_{1} + 2x_{1}x_{2}x'_{1}x'_{2} + x_{2}^{2}x'^{2}_{2}$$

**Step 5:** Distribute the $2c$ term across the inner product.

$$2c(x_{1}x'_{1} + x_{2}x'_{2}) = 2cx_{1}x'_{1} + 2cx_{2}x'_{2}$$

**Step 6:** Assemble the full expression. Note that each term is a product of an expression in $\mathbf{x}$ with the same expression in $\mathbf{x}'$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') &= x_{1}^{2}x'^{2}_{1} + 2x_{1}x_{2}x'_{1}x'_{2} + x_{2}^{2}x'^{2}_{2} \\
&\quad + 2cx_{1}x'_{1} + 2cx_{2}x'_{2} + c^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7:** Identify the feature map. Factor as $\boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x})^{\top}\boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}')$:

$$\boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} x_{1}^{2} \\ \sqrt{2}\,x_{1}x_{2} \\ x_{2}^{2} \\ \sqrt{2c}\,x_{1} \\ \sqrt{2c}\,x_{2} \\ c \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}') = \begin{bmatrix} x'^{2}_{1} \\ \sqrt{2}\,x'_{1}x'_{2} \\ x'^{2}_{2} \\ \sqrt{2c}\,x'_{1} \\ \sqrt{2c}\,x'_{2} \\ c \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 8:** Verify the inner product recovers the kernel.

$$\boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x})^{\top}\boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}') = x_{1}^{2}x'^{2}_{1} + 2x_{1}x_{2}x'_{1}x'_{2} + x_{2}^{2}x'^{2}_{2} + 2c\,x_{1}x'_{1} + 2c\,x_{2}x'_{2} + c^{2}$$

This matches Step 6 exactly, confirming $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \langle \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}), \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}') \rangle$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** For degree $p$ in $d$ dimensions, the explicit feature space has $D = \binom{d+p}{p}$ dimensions, which grows **combinatorially**. The kernel trick avoids this by computing a single scalar directly.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: The Gaussian RBF Kernel is Infinite-Dimensional

**Objective:** Show that the Gaussian RBF kernel corresponds to an infinite-dimensional feature map.

**Step 1:** Start with the RBF kernel definition.

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\vert\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}'\vert\vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Expand the squared Euclidean norm.

$$\vert\vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}'\vert\vert^{2} = \mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x} - 2\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}' + \mathbf{x}'^{\top}\mathbf{x}'$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the exponential. Set $\gamma = \dfrac{1}{2\sigma^{2}}$ for brevity.

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \exp\!\big(-\gamma\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x} + 2\gamma\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}' - \gamma\mathbf{x}'^{\top}\mathbf{x}'\big)$$

**Step 4:** Use the identity $\exp(A + B + C) = \exp(A)\exp(B)\exp(C)$.

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \exp(-\gamma\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}) \cdot \exp(2\gamma\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}') \cdot \exp(-\gamma\mathbf{x}'^{\top}\mathbf{x}')$$

**Step 5:** Expand the middle term $\exp(2\gamma\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}')$ as a Taylor series.

$$\exp(2\gamma\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}') = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{(2\gamma)^{k}}{k!} (\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}')^{k}$$

**Step 6:** Expand $(\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}')^{k}$ as a sum of monomials. For 1D ($d=1$, $x = x_1$):

$$(\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}')^{k} = \sum_{n=0}^{k} \binom{k}{n} x_{1}^{n} x'^{n}_{1} \cdot \text{(higher-order terms)}$$

**Step 7:** Collect the contribution of each monomial $x_1^n x_1'^n$ to the kernel. The coefficient of $x_1^n x_1'^n$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Coefficient of } x_1^n x_1'^n &= \exp(-\gamma x_1^2) \cdot \binom{k}{n} \cdot \dfrac{(2\gamma)^{k}}{k!} \cdot \exp(-\gamma x_1'^2) \\
&= \dfrac{1}{n!} \cdot \left( \dfrac{(2\gamma)^{n}}{n! \cdot \text{(normalization)}} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8:** The final feature map is therefore an infinite sum over Hermite polynomial basis functions, weighted by $\exp(-\gamma x_1^2)$.

$$\boldsymbol{\phi}(x_1) = \exp(-\gamma x_1^2) \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ \sqrt{2\gamma}\,x_1 \\ \dfrac{(2\gamma)^{2}}{\sqrt{2!}}\,x_1^{2} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2!}} \\ \vdots \\ \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{k!}} H_{k}(\sqrt{2\gamma}\,x_1) \\ \vdots \end{bmatrix}$$

where $H_k$ denotes the $k$-th **probabilist's Hermite polynomial**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The Gaussian RBF kernel is an **infinite-dimensional inner product**. Computing the explicit feature vector $\boldsymbol{\phi}$ is impossible in practice, yet $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}')$ is a single scalar. This is the **ultimate triumph of the kernel trick**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Distance in Feature Space

**Objective:** Express $\vert\vert \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) - \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}') \vert\vert^{2}$ purely in terms of the kernel.

**Step 1:** Expand the squared norm using the inner product property.

$$\vert\vert \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) - \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}') \vert\vert^{2} = \langle \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) - \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}'), \, \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) - \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}') \rangle$$

**Step 2:** Apply bilinearity of the inner product.

$$= \langle \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}), \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) \rangle - 2\langle \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}), \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}') \rangle + \langle \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}'), \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}') \rangle$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the kernel definition $K(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}) = \langle \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{a}), \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{b}) \rangle$.

$$\vert\vert \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) - \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}') \vert\vert^{2} = K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}) - 2K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') + K(\mathbf{x}', \mathbf{x}')$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Use-Case:** This identity enables **nonlinear clustering, k-NN in feature space, and Radial Basis Function networks** — all without ever computing $\boldsymbol{\phi}$ explicitly.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: The Dual of Kernel Ridge Regression

**Objective:** Transform Kernel Ridge Regression (KRR) into a kernelized dual form.

**Primal KRR Objective:**

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}} \; \vert\vert \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{y} \vert\vert^{2} + \lambda \vert\vert \mathbf{w} \vert\vert^{2}$$

**Step 1:** Set the gradient to zero.

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L} = 2\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{w} - 2\mathbf{X}\mathbf{y} + 2\lambda\mathbf{w} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $\mathbf{w}$ (the representer theorem guarantees $\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\alpha}$).

$$(\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}^{\top} + \lambda \mathbf{I})\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{y}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\alpha}$.

$$(\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}^{\top} + \lambda \mathbf{I})\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\alpha} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{y}$$

**Step 4:** Identify the kernel (Gram) matrix $\mathbf{G} = \mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}$ (linear case) and rearrange.

$$\lambda \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\alpha} + \mathbf{X}\mathbf{G}\boldsymbol{\alpha} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{y}$$

**Step 5:** Pre-multiply by $\mathbf{X}^{\top}$ to obtain the dual.

$$(\mathbf{G} + \lambda \mathbf{I})\boldsymbol{\alpha} = \mathbf{y}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for the dual coefficients.

$$\boldsymbol{\alpha} = (\mathbf{G} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{y}$$

**Step 7:** Prediction for a new test point $\mathbf{z}$ uses the kernel:

$$f(\mathbf{z}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{z} = \boldsymbol{\alpha}^{\top}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{z} = \boldsymbol{\alpha}^{\top} \mathbf{k}_{\mathbf{z}}$$

where $\mathbf{k}_{\mathbf{z}} = [K(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{z}), \dots, K(\mathbf{x}_N, \mathbf{z})]^{\top}$.

**Step 8:** Replace $\mathbf{G}$ with the kernelized Gram matrix $\mathbf{K}_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ to get the **nonlinear KRR predictor**.

$$f(\mathbf{z}) = \mathbf{y}^{\top}(\mathbf{K} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} \boldsymbol{\kappa}(\mathbf{z})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result (KTU Board-Favorite Formula):**
> $$\boxed{f(\mathbf{z}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_{i} K(\mathbf{x}_{i}, \mathbf{z}), \quad \boldsymbol{\alpha} = (\mathbf{K} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} \mathbf{y}}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Kernels

| Property | Linear | Polynomial | Gaussian RBF | Sigmoid |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mapping dimension** | $d$ | $\binom{d+p}{p}$ | $\infty$ | Not strictly valid |
| **PSD guarantee** | Yes | Yes (if $c \geq 0$) | Always | No |
| **Universal approximator** | No | No (fixed degree) | Yes (dense in $C(\mathcal{X})$) | No |
| **Hyperparameter sensitivity** | None | Low | High ($\sigma$) | High |
| **Computational cost** | $O(d)$ | $O(d)$ | $O(d)$ | $O(d)$ |
| **Decision boundary** | Hyperplane | Polynomial curve | Smooth closed region | Unstable |
| **KTU Exam frequency** | High | High | Very High | Medium |
