$$\frac{\partial L^{(T)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} = \frac{\partial L^{(T)}}{\partial h^{(T)}} \cdot \frac{\partial h^{(T)}}{\partial h^{(t)}}$$

The composite term can be expanded:
$$\frac{\partial h^{(T)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} = \frac{\partial h^{(T)}}{\partial h^{(T-1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial h^{(T-1)}}{\partial h^{(T-2)}} \cdots \frac{\partial h^{(t+1)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\partial h^{(k+1)}}{\partial h^{(k)}}$$

Each factor is:
$$\frac{\partial h^{(k+1)}}{\partial h^{(k)}} = W_{hh}^{\top}$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{\partial h^{(T)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} = \left(W_{hh}^{\top}\right)^{T-t}$$

### 3.1.3 Spectral Decomposition Analysis

Let $W_{hh}$ have eigendecomposition $W_{hh} = Q \Lambda Q^{-1}$, where $\Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \dots, \lambda_{n_h})$ is the diagonal matrix of eigenvalues $\lambda_i$. Then:
$$\left(W_{hh}^{\top}\right)^{T-t} = \left(Q^{-\top} \Lambda Q^{\top}\right)^{T-t} = Q^{-\top} \Lambda^{T-t} Q^{\top}$$

The **spectral radius** is defined as $\rho(W_{hh}) = \max_i \mid \lambda_i \mid$.

**Case 1 — Vanishing Gradient:** If $\rho(W_{hh}) < 1$, then $\mid \lambda_i \mid < 1$ for all $i$, so $\mid \lambda_i \mid^{T-t} \to 0$ as $T - t \to \infty$.

**Case 2 — Exploding Gradient:** If $\rho(W_{hh}) > 1$, then $\mid \lambda_i \mid^{T-t} \to \infty$ as $T - t \to \infty$.

With the $\tanh$ activation, the Jacobian is further multiplied by $\text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(\cdot)) \leq 1$, making the effective spectral radius even smaller and worsening vanishing.

### 3.1.4 Conclusion

The gradient norm behaves asymptotically as:
$$\left\Vert \frac{\partial h^{(T)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} \right\Vert \;\sim\; \mathcal{O}\!\left(\rho(W_{hh})^{T-t}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **theoretical foundation** of the vanishing gradient problem. LSTM solves it by replacing the multiplicative chain with an **additive** cell-state update, creating a near-constant gradient highway.

## 3.2 Derivation: LSTM Gradient Highway

We show that the LSTM cell-state gradient does not vanish exponentially.

### 3.2.1 Backprop Through Cell State

From the cell-state update $C^{(t)} = f^{(t)} \odot C^{(t-1)} + i^{(t)} \odot \tilde{C}^{(t)}$, the gradient is:
$$\frac{\partial C^{(t)}}{\partial C^{(t-1)}} = f^{(t)\top} + \frac{\partial f^{(t)}}{\partial C^{(t-1)}} C^{(t-1)} + \frac{\partial i^{(t)}}{\partial C^{(t-1)}} \tilde{C}^{(t)} + \frac{\partial \tilde{C}^{(t)}}{\partial C^{(t-1)}} i^{(t)}$$

### 3.2.2 Dominant Term Analysis

The **dominant** term in the above expression is $f^{(t)\top}$ because the other terms are second-order and involve derivatives of the gate functions (which depend on $h^{(t-1)}$ via small weights). Therefore, approximately:
$$\frac{\partial C^{(t)}}{\partial C^{(t-1)}} \approx f^{(t)}$$

Iterating over $T - t$ steps:
$$\frac{\partial C^{(T)}}{\partial C^{(t)}} \approx \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} f^{(k)}$$

### 3.2.3 The Learnable Forgetting Factor

Unlike vanilla RNN where $\rho(W_{hh})$ is **fixed and uncontrolled**, the LSTM has the **forget gate** $f^{(k)}$ as a **learnable vector** with values in $[0, 1]$. The network can learn to set $f^{(k)} \approx 1$ for important time steps, ensuring the gradient product remains near **1** and avoiding vanishing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **central insight** of LSTM: by making the gradient multiplicative factor **adaptive and learnable**, the network can selectively preserve gradient flow, solving the vanishing gradient problem for long sequences.

## 3.3 Numerical Example: Forward Pass in a Toy RNN

**Problem Setup:** Consider an RNN with $n_x = 1$ (1 input), $n_h = 2$ (2 hidden units). Given:
$$W_{xh} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad W_{hh} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0.2 \\ 0.3 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad b_h = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Input sequence: $x^{(1)} = 1, \, x^{(2)} = 0.5, \, x^{(3)} = 0.8$. Initial $h^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$.

### Time Step $t = 1$

$$a^{(1)} = W_{xh} x^{(1)} + W_{hh} h^{(0)} + b_h = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix}(1) + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0.2 \\ 0.3 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$h^{(1)} = \tanh\!\left(\begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4621 \\ 0.2913 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Time Step $t = 2$

$$a^{(2)} = W_{xh} x^{(2)} + W_{hh} h^{(1)} + b_h = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix}(0.5) + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0.2 \\ 0.3 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0.4621 \\ 0.2913 \end{bmatrix}$$

Computing each component:
$$a^{(2)}_1 = 0.5 \times 0.5 + (0.1)(0.4621) + (0.2)(0.2913) = 0.25 + 0.0462 + 0.0583 = 0.3545$$

$$a^{(2)}_2 = 0.3 \times 0.5 + (0.3)(0.4621) + (0.1)(0.2913) = 0.15 + 0.1386 + 0.0291 = 0.3177$$

$$h^{(2)} = \tanh\!\left(\begin{bmatrix} 0.3545 \\ 0.3177 \end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3401 \\ 0.3085 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Time Step $t = 3$

$$a^{(3)} = W_{xh} x^{(3)} + W_{hh} h^{(2)} + b_h = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix}(0.8) + \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 & 0.2 \\ 0.3 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0.3401 \\ 0.3085 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$a^{(3)}_1 = 0.4 + (0.1)(0.3401) + (0.2)(0.3085) = 0.4 + 0.0340 + 0.0617 = 0.4957$$

$$a^{(3)}_2 = 0.24 + (0.3)(0.3401) + (0.1)(0.3085) = 0.24 + 0.1020 + 0.0309 = 0.3729$$

$$h^{(3)} = \tanh\!\left(\begin{bmatrix} 0.4957 \\ 0.3729 \end{bmatrix}\right) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4588 \\ 0.3556 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Observe how the hidden state smoothly **propagates** the influence of past inputs forward — the recurrent matrix $W_{hh}$ acts as a "memory filter" blending past state with new evidence.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: RNN vs LSTM vs GRU

| **Criterion** | **Vanilla RNN** | **LSTM** | **GRU** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gates | 0 | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| Cell state | $h^{(t)}$ only | $C^{(t)}$ + $h^{(t)}$ | $h^{(t)}$ only |
| Memory mechanism | Hidden recurrence | Additive cell state | Linear interpolation of hidden |
| Number of parameters | $4(n_h^2 + n_h n_x + n_h)$ | $4(n_h^2 + n_h n_x + n_h)$ | $3(n_h^2 + n_h n_x + n_h)$ |
| Vanishing gradient | Severe | Solved (gradient highway) | Mostly solved |
| Training speed | Fastest | Slowest (most params) | Moderate |
| Long-sequence performance | Poor | Excellent | Good (often comparable) |
| Best use case | Short sequences, simple patterns | Long sequences, complex deps | Moderate sequences, faster training |
| Introduced by | Rumelhart et al. (1986) | Hochreiter & Schmidhuber (1997) | Cho et al. (2014) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The parameter count formula uses $n_h$ = hidden size, $n_x$ = input size. **GRU uses ~25% fewer parameters than LSTM** because it has only 2 gates vs 3.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Vanilla RNN — Unrolled Computational Graph

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1["x t-2"] --> nodeA1["h t-2"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA2["h t-1"]
    X2["x t-1"] --> nodeA2
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3["h t"]
    X3["x t"] --> nodeA3
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4["h t+1"]
    X4["x t+1"] --> nodeA4
    nodeA1 --> Y1["y t-2"]
    nodeA2 --> Y2["y t-1"]
    nodeA3 --> Y3["y t"]
    nodeA4 --> Y4["y t+1"]
```

**Block-Level Architecture Interpretation:**
- Each rectangular block is the **same RNN cell** unrolled across time.
- Horizontal arrows between $h$ blocks represent the **recurrent weight** $W_{hh}$.
- Vertical arrows represent the **input weight** $W_{xh}$.
- Output arrows represent $W_{hy}$.
- **Parameter sharing** is implicit: the same $W$ matrices are reused at every time step.

## 4.2 LSTM — Detailed Cell Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INP_X["x t"] --> CONCAT1["Concat h t-1, x t"]
    INP_H["h t-1"] --> CONCAT1
    CONCAT1 --> FG["Forget Gate sigmoid"]
    CONCAT1 --> IG["Input Gate sigmoid"]
    CONCAT1 --> CAND["Candidate Cell tanh"]
    CONCAT1 --> OG["Output Gate sigmoid"]
    C_PREV["C t-1"] --> MULT1["Element-wise Product"]
    FG --> MULT1
    MULT1 --> ADD["Addition"]
    IG --> MULT2["Element-wise Product"]
    CAND --> MULT2
    MULT2 --> ADD
    ADD --> C_NEW["C t"]
    ADD --> TANH["tanh"]
    OG --> MULT3["Element-wise Product"]
    TANH --> MULT3
    MULT3 --> H_NEW["h t"]
    C_NEW --> NEXT1["To Next Time Step"]
    H_NEW --> OUT["Output y t"]
```

**Architecture Interpretation:**
- The **top horizontal line** ($C^{(t-1)} \to C^{(t)}$) is the **gradient highway** that solves vanishing gradients.
- The **three gates** (forget, input, output) act as **analog control valves** with continuous values in $[0, 1]$.
- The **cell state** is the long-term memory; the **hidden state** is the working/short-term output.

## 4.3 GRU — Simplified Gated Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INP_X["x t"] --> CONCAT1["Concat h t-1, x t"]
    INP_H["h t-1"] --> CONCAT1
    CONCAT1 --> ZG["Update Gate z t sigmoid"]
    CONCAT1 --> RG["Reset Gate r t sigmoid"]
    ZG --> SUB["1 minus z t"]
    RG --> MULT1["Element-wise Product with h t-1"]
    INP_H --> MULT1
    MULT1 --> CONCAT2["Concat with x t"]
    INP_X --> CONCAT2
    CONCAT2 --> CAND["Candidate h tilde t tanh"]
    CAND --> MULT2["Element-wise Product with z t"]
    ZG --> MULT2
    SUB --> MULT3["Element-wise Product with h t-1"]
    INP_H --> MULT3
    MULT2 --> ADD["Addition"]
    MULT3 --> ADD
    ADD --> H_NEW["h t"]
    H_NEW --> OUT["Output y t"]
    H_NEW --> NEXT1["To Next Time Step"]
```

**Architecture Interpretation:**
- Only **two gates** — fewer parameters than LSTM.
- The **update gate** $z^{(t)}$ performs an **interpolation** between the previous hidden state and the new candidate: $h^{(t)} = (1-z) h^{(t-1)} + z \tilde{h}^{(t)}$.
- The **reset gate** $r^{(t)}$ controls how much of $h^{(t-1)}$ is fed to the candidate computation.

## 4.4 BPTT — Unrolled Backward Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LOSS["Loss L T"] --> GRAD1["dL dh T"]
    GRAD1 --> CHAIN1["W hh Transpose"]
    CHAIN1 --> GRAD2["dL dh T-1"]
    GRAD2 --> CHAIN2["W hh Transpose"]
    CHAIN2 --> GRAD3["dL dh T-2"]
    GRAD3 --> CHAIN3["W hh Transpose"]
    CHAIN3 --> GRAD4["dL dh T-3"]
    CHAIN4["..."] --> CHAIN3
    CHAIN5["..."] --> CHAIN2
    CHAIN6["..."] --> CHAIN1
```

**Interpretation:** The repeated multiplication by $W_{hh}^{\top}$ along the backward path is the **structural cause** of vanishing/exploding gradients. LSTM replaces this chain with the cell-state additive path.

## 4.5 High-Level System Block Diagram — Sequence-to-Sequence Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SEQ["Input Sequence x1, x2, ..., xT"] --> EMBED["Embedding Layer"]
    EMBED --> ENC["Encoder LSTM or GRU Stack"]
    ENC --> CTX["Context Vector h T"]
    CTX --> DEC["Decoder LSTM or GRU Stack"]
    DEC --> OUT["Output Sequence y1, y2, ..., yT prime"]
```

**Interpretation:** This is the canonical **Seq2Seq (Sutskever et al., 2014)** architecture used in machine translation, where an encoder summarizes the input into a context vector and a decoder generates the output sequence.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between feedforward neural networks and recurrent neural networks. List two applications of RNN.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Feature** | **Feedforward NN** | **Recurrent NN** |
|---|---|---|
| Information flow | Unidirectional (input → output) | Cyclic (output feeds back as input) |
| Memory | No memory of past inputs | Maintains hidden state as memory |
| Input type | Independent samples | Sequential / time-series data |
| Parameter sharing | None across layers | Same weights reused across time |
| Suitable for | Static patterns (images, tabular) | Temporal patterns (text, speech) |

**Applications of RNN (any 2):**
1. **Machine Translation** — translating sentences from one language to another (e.g., English → Malayalam).
2. **Speech Recognition** — converting spoken audio waveforms into text transcripts.
3. **Stock Price Prediction** — forecasting future stock values from historical time-series data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** A clear **tabular comparison** with at least 3 differentiating points scores full marks. Applications must be **specific to RNN** (not generic ML applications).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the vanishing gradient problem in RNNs? How does LSTM overcome it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Vanishing Gradient Problem:**
During Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT), gradients are multiplied by the recurrent weight matrix $W_{hh}$ repeatedly across time steps. If the spectral radius $\rho(W_{hh}) < 1$, the gradient shrinks **exponentially** with time-step distance, becoming numerically zero. This prevents the network from learning **long-term dependencies** (i.e., relationships between inputs far apart in the sequence).

**How LSTM overcomes it:**
LSTM introduces a **cell state** $C^{(t)}$ updated via an **additive** mechanism: $C^{(t)} = f^{(t)} \odot C^{(t-1)} + i^{(t)} \odot \tilde{C}^{(t)}$. The **forget gate** $f^{(t)}$ is a learnable vector in $[0, 1]$ that the network can train to be close to 1, creating a near-constant **gradient highway** through time. This prevents exponential decay of gradients.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Mentioning the **multiplicative vs additive** distinction explicitly is the key phrase examiners look for.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q1. (a)** Explain the architecture and forward pass equations of a **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** network with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each of the three gates. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture Overview (2 marks):**
LSTM is a gated variant of RNN designed to learn long-term dependencies. Each LSTM cell contains:
- A **cell state** $C^{(t)}$ — the long-term memory conveyor belt.
- A **hidden state** $h^{(t)}$ — the short-term working memory / output.
- **Three sigmoid gates** (forget, input, output) — control information flow.

**Role of Each Gate (3 marks):**

1. **Forget Gate $f^{(t)}$:** Acts as a "memory eraser". It decides what information to **discard** from the cell state. For example, in language modeling, when a new subject is encountered, the gender/number of the old subject can be forgotten.

2. **Input Gate $i^{(t)}$:** Acts as a "memory writer". It decides which **new** information to store in the cell state and updates it via the candidate $\tilde{C}^{(t)}$.

3. **Output Gate $o^{(t)}$:** Acts as a "memory reader". It decides what parts of the cell state to **expose** as the hidden state output $h^{(t)}$.

**Forward Pass Equations (2 marks):**

$$f^{(t)} = \sigma(W_f [h^{(t-1)}, x^{(t)}] + b_f)$$
$$i^{(t)} = \sigma(W_i [h^{(t-1)}, x^{(t)}] + b_i)$$
$$\tilde{C}^{(t)} = \tanh(W_C [h^{(t-1)}, x^{(t)}] + b_C)$$
$$C^{(t)} = f^{(t)} \odot C^{(t-1)} + i^{(t)} \odot \tilde{C}^{(t)}$$
$$o^{(t)} = \sigma(W_o [h^{(t-1)}, x^{(t)}] + b_o)$$
$$h^{(t)} = o^{(t)} \odot \tanh(C^{(t)})$$

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.2):** The cell-state highway runs horizontally, with the three gates regulating the vertical flows.

---

**Q1. (b)** Derive the gradient of the loss with respect to the hidden state of a vanilla RNN across multiple time steps and show mathematically why gradients vanish. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Setup (1 mark):**
Consider a vanilla RNN with hidden update $h^{(t)} = W_{hh} h^{(t-1)} + W_{xh} x^{(t)}$ and loss $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L^{(t)}$ at the final time step. For simplicity, ignore the $\tanh$ activation (the same argument holds with the bounded derivative).

**Chain Rule Derivation (3 marks):**
We want to compute $\frac{\partial L^{(T)}}{\partial h^{(t)}}$ for $t < T$:
$$\frac{\partial L^{(T)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} = \frac{\partial L^{(T)}}{\partial h^{(T)}} \cdot \frac{\partial h^{(T)}}{\partial h^{(t)}}$$

The composite Jacobian unrolls as:
$$\frac{\partial h^{(T)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} \frac{\partial h^{(k+1)}}{\partial h^{(k)}} = \prod_{k=t}^{T-1} W_{hh}^{\top} = \left(W_{hh}^{\top}\right)^{T-t}$$

**Spectral Analysis (2 marks):**
Let $W_{hh} = Q \Lambda Q^{-1}$ be the eigendecomposition. Then:
$$\left(W_{hh}^{\top}\right)^{T-t} = Q^{-\top} \Lambda^{T-t} Q^{\top}$$

If the spectral radius $\rho(W_{hh}) = \max_i \mid \lambda_i \mid < 1$, then $\mid \lambda_i \mid^{T-t} \to 0$ exponentially as $T-t$ increases. The gradient norm is bounded by:
$$\left\Vert \frac{\partial h^{(T)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} \right\Vert \leq \rho(W_{hh})^{T-t} \cdot \kappa(Q)^2$$

where $\kappa(Q)$ is the condition number. As $T-t$ grows, this product **vanishes to zero**.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The gradient decays exponentially with the time-step distance $T-t$, making it numerically impossible to learn dependencies beyond ~10 time steps. This is the **vanishing gradient problem**, which LSTM solves via the additive cell-state update.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU Model Paper 2024]

**Q2. (a)** Compare **LSTM** and **GRU** architectures in detail. State the equations of GRU and explain its two gates. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| **Feature** | **LSTM** | **GRU** |
|---|---|---|
| Number of gates | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| State variables | Cell state $C^{(t)}$ + hidden state $h^{(t)}$ | Only hidden state $h^{(t)}$ |
| Output mechanism | $h^{(t)} = o^{(t)} \odot \tanh(C^{(t)})$ | $h^{(t)} = (1-z^{(t)})h^{(t-1)} + z^{(t)}\tilde{h}^{(t)}$ |
| Memory control | Explicit via cell-state write | Implicit via interpolation |
| Parameters | $4(n_h^2 + n_h n_x + n_h)$ | $3(n_h^2 + n_h n_x + n_h)$ |
| Training speed | Slower | ~25% faster |
| Long-sequence performance | Excellent | Good (often comparable) |

**GRU Equations (2 marks):**
$$z^{(t)} = \sigma(W_z [h^{(t-1)}, x^{(t)}] + b_z)$$
$$r^{(t)} = \sigma(W_r [h^{(t-1)}, x^{(t)}] + b_r)$$
$$\tilde{h}^{(t)} = \tanh(W_h [r^{(t)} \odot h^{(t-1)}, x^{(t)}] + b_h)$$
$$h^{(t)} = (1 - z^{(t)}) \odot h^{(t-1)} + z^{(t)} \odot \tilde{h}^{(t)}$$

**Explanation of the Two Gates (2 marks):**
- **Update Gate $z^{(t)}$:** Controls how much of the **past** hidden state to retain vs. how much of the **new** candidate to incorporate. It is a single gate that simultaneously performs the roles of LSTM's forget and input gates.
- **Reset Gate $r^{(t)}$:** Controls how much of the **past** hidden state to use when computing the new candidate $\tilde{h}^{(t)}$. When $r^{(t)} \approx 0$, the candidate ignores the past and behaves like a feedforward input transformation.

---

**Q2. (b)** Explain **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)** for a recurrent neural network with a sequence length of $T = 4$. Write the gradient equations and discuss the practical challenges. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**BPTT Algorithm Concept (2 marks):**
BPTT is the application of the backpropagation algorithm to the **unrolled** computational graph of an RNN. The network is "unrolled" for $T$ time steps, creating a deep feedforward network of depth $T$, and standard backpropagation is applied.

**Gradient Equations (3 marks):**
For loss $L = \sum_{t=1}^{4} L^{(t)}$ and hidden update $h^{(t)} = \tanh(W_{hh} h^{(t-1)} + W_{xh} x^{(t)})$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{4} \sum_{k=1}^{t} \frac{\partial L^{(t)}}{\partial h^{(t)}} \left[\prod_{j=k}^{t-1} \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(a^{(j)})\right) W_{hh}^{\top}\right] \frac{\partial h^{(k)}}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

For example, the gradient of $L^{(4)}$ w.r.t. $h^{(1)}$ is:
$$\frac{\partial L^{(4)}}{\partial h^{(1)}} = \frac{\partial L^{(4)}}{\partial h^{(4)}} \cdot D_3 W_{hh}^{\top} \cdot D_2 W_{hh}^{\top} \cdot D_1 W_{hh}^{\top}$$

where $D_k = \text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(a^{(k)}))$.

**Practical Challenges (2 marks):**
1. **Vanishing Gradients:** If $\rho(W_{hh}) < 1$, the product of 4 Jacobians shrinks exponentially, preventing early time steps from receiving useful gradient signals.
2. **Exploding Gradients:** If $\rho(W_{hh}) > 1$, the product grows exponentially, causing numerical overflow and unstable weight updates.
3. **Computational Cost:** BPTT requires storing all intermediate hidden states for $T$ steps, making memory consumption $O(T)$.
4. **Truncated BPTT** is used in practice to limit $T$ to a manageable window (e.g., 64–256 steps).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in LSTM/GRU Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing gate dimensions:** Students often write the gate equations with **wrong matrix dimensions** (e.g., transposing $W_f$). Always specify: $W_f$ has dimension $n_h \times (n_h + n_x)$, applied to the **concatenated** vector $[h^{(t-1)}; x^{(t)}]$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $\odot$ (Hadamard) product:** The cell-state update $C^{(t)} = f^{(t)} \odot C^{(t-1)} + i^{(t)} \odot \tilde{C}^{(t)}$ uses **element-wise** multiplication, not matrix multiplication. Writing $f^{(t)} C^{(t-1)}$ (matrix product) is a **deduction of 0.5–1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Confusing LSTM cell state with hidden state:** These are **two different** vectors. The cell state is the long-term memory; the hidden state is the output. Many students incorrectly use them interchangeably.
>
> 4. **Saying LSTM "solves" vanishing gradient completely:** LSTM **mitigates** vanishing gradients via the additive cell-state path but does **not** mathematically guarantee no vanishing. Use the word "**alleviates**" or "**largely overcomes**".
>
> 5. **Omitting the diagram:** A 7-mark question on LSTM/GRU architecture without a **labeled block diagram** typically loses **1–2 marks** in KTU valuation.
>
> 6. **Mixing up GRU's update and reset gate roles:** Update gate $z^{(t)}$ controls **retention vs. update**; reset gate $r^{(t)}$ controls **how much past to use in the candidate computation**. Reversing these is a common error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: RNN/LSTM/GRU**

- **RNN Core Idea:** Maintain a hidden state $h^{(t)}$ that summarizes the past; share weights across time steps.
- **BPTT:** Unroll the network for $T$ time steps, then apply backpropagation; gradients are products of Jacobians across time.
- **Vanishing Gradient Cause:** Repeated multiplication by $W_{hh}^{\top}$ causes gradient norm to decay as $\rho(W_{hh})^{T-t}$.
- **Exploding Gradient Solution:** **Gradient clipping** — rescale gradient if its norm exceeds a threshold.
- **Vanishing Gradient Solution:** **LSTM/GRU** with gating mechanisms and additive cell-state updates.
- **LSTM Three Gates:** **F**orget (discard old), **I**nput (write new), **O**utput (read out) — mnemonic: **"F.I.O."**
- **LSTM Cell State:** Updated additively: $C^{(t)} = f^{(t)} \odot C^{(t-1)} + i^{(t)} \odot \tilde{C}^{(t)}$ — this is the **gradient highway**.
- **LSTM Hidden State:** $h^{(t)} = o^{(t)} \odot \tanh(C^{(t)})$ — a filtered, exposed view of the cell state.
- **GRU Two Gates:** **U**pdate $z^{(t)}$ (interpolation), **R**eset $r^{(t)}$ (memory erasure for candidate).
- **GRU Hidden Update:** $h^{(t)} = (1 - z^{(t)}) \odot h^{(t-1)} + z^{(t)} \odot \tilde{h}^{(t)}$ — **convex combination**.
- **Parameter Count:** LSTM has 4 gates worth; GRU has 3 gates worth → GRU is **~25% smaller**.
- **Activation Functions:** **Sigmoid ($\sigma$)** for gates (output in $[0,1]$); **$\tanh$** for state values (output in $[-1,1]$); **softmax** for classification output.
- **Key Hyperparameters:** Hidden size $n_h$, sequence length $T$, learning rate, gradient clip threshold.
- **Bidirectional RNNs:** Process the sequence both forward and backward, then concatenate — captures past **and** future context (used in BERT-style models).
- **Applications to Remember:** Machine translation, speech recognition, sentiment analysis, time-series forecasting, video captioning.
- **Historical Origin:** RNN (Rumelhart, 1986) → LSTM (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997) → GRU (Cho et al., 2014).
- **Modern Relevance:** LSTMs/GRUs are the **backbone** of pre-transformer NLP and remain dominant in **time-series, IoT, and signal-processing** applications relevant to Kerala's industrial automation and smart-infrastructure projects.
- **Spectral Radius Criterion:** $\rho(W_{hh}) < 1 \Rightarrow$ vanishing; $\rho(W_{hh}) > 1 \Rightarrow$ exploding.
- **Truncated BPTT:** Limits the unroll length $T$ to a fixed window to bound computational cost and gradient instability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformers: BERT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT525 - ARTIFICIAL NEURAL NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Transformers: BERT ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers)** is a pre-trained deep learning model introduced by Devlin et al. (Google, 2018) that revolutionized Natural Language Processing (NLP). It is a **multi-layer bidirectional Transformer encoder** trained on two unsupervised tasks: **Masked Language Modeling (MLM)** and **Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)**. BERT learns contextualized word representations by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all attention layers, unlike previous unidirectional models. $$ \text{BERT} = \text{MultiLayer Bidirectional Transformer Encoder} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** BERT is covered under the *Transformers* unit. Students must understand the **Encoder-Only architecture**, the **Self-Attention mechanism**, **Input Embeddings** (Token + Segment + Position), and the **Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning** paradigm. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of BERT as a "Bookworm Reading a Whole Page at Once"** 📖 Imagine a student (an older NLP model like LSTM) who reads a book **one word at a time, only forwards** (left-to-right). By the time they reach the end of a sentence, they may have forgotten the beginning. Now imagine **BERT as an extremely intelligent bookworm** that reads the **entire sentence simultaneously**, with **attention spotlighting** every word in relation to every other word. When BERT sees the word *"bank"* in *"I deposited money in the bank"*, it instantly considers *money* and *deposited* (right context) **and** *I* and *in* (left context) to understand the meaning. This **bidirectional, simultaneous reading** is what makes BERT so powerful for language understanding. | Old Models (RNN/LSTM) | BERT (Transformer Encoder) | |---|---| | Sequential, one word at a time | Parallel, all words at once | | Unidirectional (L→R or R→L) | **Bidirectional** (L←R) | | Static embeddings (Word2Vec) | **Contextualized** embeddings | | Task-specific training from scratch | **Pre-train then Fine-tune** | ### 1.3 Why BERT Matters — Key Characteristics - **Bidirectional Context**: Understands a word using both past and future context simultaneously. - **Pre-trained on Huge Corpora**: Trained on **3.3 Billion words** (Wikipedia + BookCorpus). - **Transfer Learning Pioneer**: One pre-trained model → fine-tuned for **11+ NLP tasks** (QA, sentiment, NER). - **Architecture Sizes**: - **BERT-Base**: 12 layers, 768 hidden, 12 heads, **110M parameters** - **BERT-Large**: 24 layers, 1024 hidden, 16 heads, **340M parameters** > [!TIP] > **Key Metric to Remember:** BERT-Base has **110 million parameters** and BERT-Large has **340 million parameters** — a favourite KTU 2-mark question! ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Transformer Encoder — Foundation of BERT BERT uses **only the encoder half** of the original Vaswani et al. (2017) Transformer. Each encoder layer has two sub-layers: 1. **Multi-Head Self-Attention** 2. **Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN)** Each sub-layer uses a **residual connection** followed by **Layer Normalization**. ### 2.2 Self-Attention Mechanism (Scaled Dot-Product Attention) The heart of BERT. For each input token, the model computes three vectors by multiplying the input embedding with learned weight matrices: $$ Q = X W^Q, \quad K = X W^K, \quad V = X W^V $$ where: - $Q$ = **Query** vector (dimension $d_k$) - $K$ = **Key** vector (dimension $d_k$) - $V$ = **Value** vector (dimension $d_v$) - $X$ = Input embeddings (matrix of all tokens) The **Scaled Dot-Product Attention** is computed as: $$ \text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V $$ The scaling factor $\sqrt{d_k}$ prevents the dot products from growing too large in high dimensions, which would push the softmax into regions with extremely small gradients. > [!NOTE] > **Why $\sqrt{d_k}$?** As $d_k$ grows, the variance of $QK^{\top}$ grows, pushing softmax into saturated regions. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the variance back to ~1, keeping gradients healthy. ### 2.3 Multi-Head Attention Instead of performing a single attention function, BERT projects $Q$, $K$, $V$ into **$h$ different sub-spaces** and runs attention in parallel: $$ \text{MultiHead}(Q, K, V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h)\, W^O $$ where each head is: $$ \text{head}_i = \text{Attention}(Q W_i^Q,\ K_i^K,\ V_i^V) $$ For BERT-Base: $h = 12$ heads, $d_k = d_v = d_{\text{model}}/h = 64$. ### 2.4 Position-wise Feed-Forward Network (FFN) Applied independently to each position: $$ \text{FFN}(x) = \max(0,\ x W_1 + b_1)\, W_2 + b_2 $$ This is a two-layer MLP with a **ReLU** activation in between, projecting from $d_{\text{model}} \rightarrow d_{ff} \rightarrow d_{\text{model}}$. For BERT-Base: $d_{\text{model}} = 768$, $d_{ff} = 3072$. ### 2.5 BERT Input Representation Each input token is represented as the **sum of three embeddings**: $$ E_{\text{input}} = E_{\text{token}} + E_{\text{segment}} + E_{\text{position}} $$ | Embedding Type | Purpose | Vocabulary | |---|---|---| | **Token Embedding** | WordPiece subword units | ~30,000 tokens | | **Segment Embedding** | Distinguishes Sentence A vs Sentence B | 2 vectors (EA, EB) | | **Position Embedding** | Encodes token position (max 512) | 512 learned vectors | > [!IMPORTANT] > BERT also adds two **special tokens**: > - `[CLS]` — Classification token (output used for sentence-level tasks) > - `[SEP]` — Separator token (marks end of each sentence) ### 2.6 Pre-training Tasks #### Task 1: Masked Language Modeling (MLM) - **15% of tokens** are randomly selected and replaced: - 80% → replaced with `[MASK]` - 10% → replaced with random token - 10% → left unchanged - Model predicts the **original vocabulary ID** of the masked token using cross-entropy loss. #### Task 2: Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) - Given sentence pairs (A, B), model predicts whether B is the **actual next sentence** of A. - Output: Binary classification using the `[CLS]` token's final hidden state. - Training data: 50% true pairs, 50% random pairs. ### 2.7 Fine-tuning Paradigm After pre-training, BERT is fine-tuned on downstream tasks by adding a single task-specific output layer: $$ P(y \mid x) = \text{softmax}(W_{\text{task}} \cdot h_{[\text{CLS}]}) $$ where $h_{[\text{CLS}]}$ is the final hidden state of the `[CLS]` token. > [!TIP] > **Fine-tuning is fast** — typically **2–4 epochs** with a small learning rate ($2 \times 10^{-5}$ to $5 \times 10^{-5}$). All layers are updated. ### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Value | Notes | |---|---|---| | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | $\text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$ | Core of self-attention | | Multi-Head Concatenation | $\text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,...,\text{head}_h)W^O$ | $h$ parallel heads | | Input Embedding | $E_{\text{token}} + E_{\text{segment}} + E_{\text{position}}$ | Sum of 3 embeddings | | BERT-Base Layers ($L$) | $12$ | Transformer blocks | | BERT-Base Hidden Size ($d_{\text{model}}$) | $768$ | Embedding dimension | | BERT-Base Heads ($h$) | $12$ | Attention heads | | BERT-Base FFN Size ($d_{ff}$) | $3072$ | Inner FFN dimension | | BERT-Base Parameters | $\approx 110\,\text{M}$ | Total parameters | | BERT-Large Parameters | $\approx 340\,\text{M}$ | Total parameters | | Max Sequence Length | $512$ tokens | Positional limit | | MLM Mask Probability | $15\%$ | Pre-training only | | MLM Replacement (Mask) | $80\%$ → `[MASK]` | Of 15% selected | | MLM Replacement (Random) | $10\%$ | Of 15% selected | | MLM Replacement (Keep) | $10\%$ | Of 15% selected | | Recommended LR (Fine-tune) | $2\text{e-}5$ to $5\text{e-}5$ | Adam optimizer | | Training Corpus Size | $3.3\,\text{B}$ words | Wikipedia + BookCorpus | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Scaled Dot-Product Attention Score **Goal:** Derive why we divide by $\sqrt{d_k}$ in the attention formula. **Step 1 — Setup the dot product.** Assume the components of $q$ and $k$ are independent random variables with mean $0$ and variance $1$. $$ q \cdot k = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} q_i k_i $$ **Step 2 — Compute the mean of the dot product.** $$ E[q \cdot k] = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} E[q_i]\, E[k_i] = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} (0)(0) = 0 $$ **Step 3 — Compute the variance of the dot product.** $$ \text{Var}(q \cdot k) = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} \text{Var}(q_i k_i) $$ Since $q_i$ and $k_i$ are independent, each with mean $0$ and variance $1$: $$ \text{Var}(q_i k_i) = E[q_i^2 k_i^2] - (E[q_i k_i])^2 = (1)(1) - 0 = 1 $$ Therefore: $$ \text{Var}(q \cdot k) = \sum_{i=1}^{d_k} 1 = d_k $$ **Step 4 — Standard deviation.** $$ \text{Std}(q \cdot k) = \sqrt{d_k} $$ **Step 5 — Normalize.** Dividing the dot product by its standard deviation gives a quantity with **unit variance**: $$ \frac{q \cdot k}{\sqrt{d_k}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Var} = 1 $$ This is the derivation of the scaling factor used in the attention formula: $$ \text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V $$ > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Without scaling, for $d_k = 64$, the dot products have std $= 8$, pushing softmax into saturation (gradient ≈ 0). Scaling keeps gradients flowing. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Parameter Count of BERT-Base We compute the total trainable parameters of BERT-Base (in millions) to verify the **110M** figure. **Step 1 — Token Embeddings.** $$ \text{Vocab} \times d_{\text{model}} = 30{,}000 \times 768 = 23{,}040{,}000 $$ **Step 2 — Position Embeddings.** $$ 512 \times 768 = 393{,}216 $$ **Step 3 — Segment Embeddings.** $$ 2 \times 768 = 1{,}536 $$ **Step 4 — Per-layer Multi-Head Attention parameters.** For $h = 12$ heads, $d_k = d_v = 64$, $d_{\text{model}} = 768$: $$ W^Q, W^K, W^V : 3 \times (768 \times 768) = 1{,}769{,}472 $$ $$ W^O : 768 \times 768 = 589{,}824 $$ $$ \text{Total per layer} = 1{,}769{,}472 + 589{,}824 = 2{,}359{,}296 $$ **Step 5 — Per-layer FFN parameters.** $$ W_1 : 768 \times 3072 = 2{,}359{,}296 $$ $$ W_2 : 3072 \times 768 = 2{,}359{,}296 $$ $$ \text{Total FFN per layer} = 4{,}718{,}592 $$ **Step 6 — Per-layer LayerNorm parameters.** $$ 2 \times (768 + 768) = 3{,}072 \quad (\gamma, \beta \text{ for two sub-layers}) $$ **Step 7 — Total per Transformer block.** $$ 2{,}359{,}296 + 4{,}718{,}592 + 3{,}072 = 7{,}080{,}960 $$ **Step 8 — 12 encoder layers.** $$ 12 \times 7{,}080{,}960 = 84{,}971{,}520 $$ **Step 9 — Pooler layer (for [CLS] classification).** $$ 768 \times 768 + 768 = 590{,}592 $$ **Step 10 — Grand Total.** $$ 23{,}040{,}000 + 393{,}216 + 1{,}536 + 84{,}971{,}520 + 590{,}592 = 108{,}996{,}864 $$ $$ \approx \mathbf{110\,\text{million parameters}} \quad \blacksquare $$ --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: BERT vs Other Models | Feature | Word2Vec (2013) | ELMo (2018) | GPT-1 (2018) | **BERT-Base (2018)** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Direction | None (static) | Bidirectional (shallow) | Unidirectional (L→R) | **Bidirectional (deep)** | | Contextualized? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ **Yes** | | Architecture | Shallow NN | Bidirectional LSTM | Transformer Decoder | **Transformer Encoder** | | Pre-training | Word co-occurrence | BiLM (forward + backward LM) | Left-to-right LM | **MLM + NSP** | | Parameters | ~300M (Google News) | ~93M | ~117M | **~110M** | | Best At | Word similarity | Feature extraction | Text generation | **Language understanding** | | Position Encoding | None | None (RNN inherent) | Learned | **Learned** | ### 3.4 Mathematical Formulation of MLM Loss For a sequence of $N$ tokens where $M$ are masked, the MLM loss is: $$ L_{\text{MLM}}(\theta) = -\sum_{i \in \text{masked}} \log P(x_i \mid x_{\setminus i};\, \theta) $$ where $x_{\setminus i}$ denotes all tokens except the masked one, and: $$ P(x_i \mid x_{\setminus i}) = \frac{\exp(H_i \cdot e(x_i)^{\top})}{\sum_{v=1}^{\vert V \vert} \exp(H_i \cdot e(v)^{\top})} $$ where: - $H_i$ = final hidden state of masked position $i$ - $e(x_i)$ = embedding of original token - $\vert V \vert$ = vocabulary size (~30,000) > [!TIP] > This is a **softmax cross-entropy loss** computed only at the masked positions (not the full sequence). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level BERT Architecture ```mermaid graph TD inputA["Input Tokens:
[CLS] My cat is [MASK] [SEP]"] inputB["Segment A / B Embeddings"] inputC["Position Embeddings 0 to N"] sumNode["Element-wise Sum
E_token + E_segment + E_position"] enc1["Encoder Layer 1
Multi-Head Self-Attention + FFN"] enc2["Encoder Layer 2"] encN["Encoder Layer N
N = 12 (Base) or 24 (Large)"] out1["Output H_CLS - Classification"] out2["Output H_i - Token Predictions MLM"] out3["Output H_CLS - NSP Binary"] inputA --> sumNode inputB --> sumNode inputC --> sumNode sumNode --> enc1 enc1 --> enc2 enc2 --> encN encN --> out1 encN --> out2 encN --> out3 ``` ### 4.2 Single Transformer Encoder Block (Internal View) ```mermaid graph TD inpX["Input X
Shape: seq_len x d_model"] attn["Multi-Head
Self-Attention
12 heads"] addNorm1["Add and
LayerNorm"] ffn["Feed-Forward
Network
d_model to d_ff to d_model"] addNorm2["Add and
LayerNorm"] outX["Output X
Same shape as input"] inpX --> attn attn --> addNorm1 inpX --> addNorm1 addNorm1 --> ffn ffn --> addNorm2 addNorm1 --> addNorm2 addNorm2 --> outX ``` ### 4.3 Pre-training vs Fine-tuning Workflow ```mermaid graph LR subgraph PT["Pre-training Stage"] pt1["Unlabeled Text Corpus
Wikipedia + BookCorpus"] pt2["Task 1: Masked LM
Predict 15 percent masked tokens"] pt3["Task 2: Next Sentence
Prediction binary"] pt4["Pre-trained BERT
110M or 340M params"] pt1 --> pt2 pt1 --> pt3 pt2 --> pt4 pt3 --> pt4 end subgraph FT["Fine-tuning Stage"] ft1["Task-Specific Labeled Data
e.g. SST-2, SQuAD, CoLA"] ft2["Add Output Layer
e.g. softmax classifier"] ft3["Fine-tuned BERT
for specific task"] ft1 --> ft2 ft2 --> ft3 end pt4 --> ft1 ``` ### 4.4 BERT Input Embedding Composition ```mermaid graph TD tok["Token Embedding
WordPiece
Vocab 30k"] seg["Segment Embedding
EA or EB
2 vectors"] pos["Position Embedding
0 to 511
512 learned vectors"] sum["Element-wise Sum
E_token plus E_segment plus E_position"] bertIn["Final Input to BERT
Shape: seq_len x 768"] tok --> sum seg --> sum pos --> sum sum --> bertIn ``` ### 4.5 Self-Attention Q K V Flow ```mermaid graph TD xIn["Input X
seq_len x d_model"] wq["W_Q Matrix
d_model x d_k"] wk["W_K Matrix
d_model x d_k"] wv["W_V Matrix
d_model x d_v"] qVec["Q Matrix
seq_len x d_k"] kVec["K Matrix
seq_len x d_k"] vVec["V Matrix
seq_len x d_v"] dot["Q K Transpose
Dot Product
seq_len x seq_len"] scale["Divide by sqrt d_k"] smax["Softmax
row-wise"] out["Multiply by V
Output seq_len x d_v"] xIn --> wq --> qVec xIn --> wk --> kVec xIn --> wv --> vVec qVec --> dot kVec --> dot dot --> scale scale --> smax smax --> out vVec --> out ``` ### 4.6 BERT Variants Family Tree ```mermaid graph TD bert["BERT Base 2018
12L 768H 110M params"] roberta["RoBERTa 2019
No NSP, more data, dynamic masking"] distilbert["DistilBERT 2019
6L 768H 66M params distilled"] albert["ALBERT 2019
Parameter sharing 12M params"] bertlarge["BERT Large 2018
24L 1024H 340M params"] bert --> roberta bert --> distilbert bert --> albert bert --> bertlarge ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — 3 Mark Questions **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is BERT? Briefly explain its key architectural innovation.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers)** is a pre-trained language representation model developed by Google in 2018. Its key architectural innovation is the use of a **multi-layer bidirectional Transformer encoder** (originally from Vaswani et al., 2017). Unlike previous models (e.g., OpenAI GPT) that process text **left-to-right** (unidirectional), BERT reads the entire input sequence **simultaneously in both directions**, allowing every position to attend to every other position via **self-attention**. This enables deeper contextual understanding of each token using both its left and right context. BERT is pre-trained on two tasks — **Masked Language Modeling (MLM)** and **Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)** — and can be fine-tuned with just one additional output layer for various downstream NLP tasks. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for bidirectional innovation, 1 for pre-training + fine-tuning.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *List any three differences between BERT and GPT.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | BERT | GPT | |---|---|---| | **Architecture** | Transformer **Encoder** | Transformer **Decoder** | | **Directionality** | **Bidirectional** | **Unidirectional** (left-to-right) | | **Pre-training Task** | MLM + NSP | Left-to-right Language Modeling | | **Best Suited For** | **Language understanding** tasks (classification, QA) | **Language generation** tasks | | **Context Used** | Full sentence context (both sides) | Only previous tokens | *(3 marks: 1 mark per valid difference.)* --- ### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the **Self-Attention mechanism** used in the BERT architecture. Derive the Scaled Dot-Product Attention formula and justify the use of the $\sqrt{d_k}$ scaling factor. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition (2 marks):** Self-attention is a mechanism that computes a weighted representation of every token in a sequence with respect to **every other token**, allowing the model to capture long-range dependencies. For each position, three vectors are computed by projecting the input embedding $X$ using learned weight matrices: $$ Q = X W^Q, \quad K = X W^K, \quad V = X W^V $$ **Scaled Dot-Product Attention (3 marks):** The attention output is computed as: $$ \text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V $$ The steps are: 1. Compute similarity scores via dot product $Q K^{\top}$ — shape $(\text{seq\_len} \times \text{seq\_len})$. 2. Scale by $\sqrt{d_k}$ to control variance. 3. Apply softmax row-wise to get attention weights (summing to 1). 4. Multiply weights by $V$ to get the contextualized output. **Justification of $\sqrt{d_k}$ (2 marks):** Assuming $q_i$ and $k_i$ are zero-mean unit-variance independent random variables, the variance of the dot product $q \cdot k$ is $d_k$. Thus, the standard deviation is $\sqrt{d_k}$. Without scaling, large $d_k$ pushes softmax into saturated regions where gradients vanish. Dividing by $\sqrt{d_k}$ normalizes the variance to 1, preserving healthy gradient flow. --- **(b)** Explain the **Input Representation** of BERT with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of `[CLS]` and `[SEP]` special tokens. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Three Embeddings (4 marks):** Each input token is represented as the **sum of three embeddings**: $$ E_{\text{input}} = E_{\text{token}} + E_{\text{segment}} + E_{\text{position}} $$ - **Token Embedding:** Uses **WordPiece tokenization** with a ~30,000-token vocabulary. Rare words are broken into subword units. - **Segment Embedding:** Distinguishes the two sentences in a pair — `EA` for Sentence A, `EB` for Sentence B. - **Position Embedding:** Learned vectors for each of the 512 possible positions. **Special Tokens (3 marks):** - **`[CLS]`** (Classification): Added at the **start** of every input sequence. Its **final hidden state** is aggregated representation of the entire sequence and is used as input to the classification head for sentence-level tasks (e.g., sentiment analysis, NSP). - **`[SEP]`** (Separator): Inserted at the **end of each sentence** to mark sentence boundaries. The model uses it along with segment embeddings to distinguish paired sentences. **Example:** ``` [CLS] I love neural networks [SEP] They are powerful [SEP] EA EA EA EA EA EB EB EB EB 0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 ``` --- **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Explain the two **pre-training objectives** of BERT — Masked Language Modeling (MLM) and Next Sentence Prediction (NSP). Why is NSP later questioned in RoBERTa? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Masked Language Modeling — MLM (4 marks):** - **15%** of the input tokens are randomly selected for prediction. - Of these 15%: - **80%** are replaced with the `[MASK]` token. - **10%** are replaced with a **random token**. - **10%** are **left unchanged**. - The model's output hidden states at masked positions are fed into a softmax over the vocabulary to predict the original token. - The cross-entropy loss is computed only on the masked positions. - The **random replacement** trick prevents the model from learning that `[MASK]` always means "predict me", improving downstream performance when `[MASK]` is absent. **Next Sentence Prediction — NSP (2 marks):** - A **binary classification** task: given sentence pairs (A, B), predict whether B is the actual next sentence of A. - Output: probability computed from the `[CLS]` token's final hidden state. - Training data: 50% positive (real pairs) + 50% negative (random pairs). - Helps BERT understand **sentence-level relationships**, useful for tasks like Question Answering and Natural Language Inference. **Why NSP is questioned in RoBERTa (1 mark):** RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019) found that **removing NSP** slightly **improves or matches** performance on downstream tasks. The hypothesis: NSP is too easy and conflates topic prediction with coherence prediction, offering little useful signal. --- **(b)** With a block diagram, describe the **two-stage paradigm** of BERT: pre-training followed by fine-tuning. List any four downstream tasks where BERT is applied. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Two-Stage Paradigm (5 marks):** **Stage 1 — Pre-training (Unsupervised):** - Model is trained on a **massive unlabeled corpus** (Wikipedia + BookCorpus, 3.3B words). - Tasks: MLM and NSP. - The model learns general language representations. - Output: a pre-trained BERT with **~110M** (Base) or **~340M** (Large) parameters. - This stage is **computationally expensive** (took several days on 64 TPU chips). **Stage 2 — Fine-tuning (Supervised):** - Add a **single task-specific output layer** (e.g., softmax classifier, span predictor). - Train the **entire model** (or partially) on labeled data of the target task. - Uses a **small learning rate** ($2\text{e-}5$ to $5\text{e-}5$) and few epochs (2–4). - Fast and data-efficient compared to pre-training. **Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.3 above):** `Unlabeled Corpus → MLM + NSP → Pre-trained BERT → Add Output Layer → Task-Specific Data → Fine-tuned BERT` **Four Downstream Applications (2 marks):** 1. **Sentiment Analysis** (e.g., SST-2, IMDb reviews) 2. **Question Answering** (e.g., SQuAD — extract answer span) 3. **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** — token-level tagging 4. **Natural Language Inference (NLI)** — sentence pair classification 5. *(Bonus)* Text classification, semantic similarity, search ranking. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks!** > > 1. **Confusing BERT with GPT** — BERT is **Encoder-only** (bidirectional), GPT is **Decoder-only** (unidirectional). Mixing these up costs 2-3 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{d_k}$ justification** — Examiners specifically want the variance argument, not just the formula. > 3. **Wrong parameter count** — BERT-Base = **110M**, BERT-Large = **340M**. Don't confuse with GPT-3 (175B). > 4. **MLM details missing** — Always mention the **80/10/10 split** rule (mask/random/keep). Just saying "15% masked" is incomplete. > 5. **NSP purpose unclear** — Many students say NSP is for "next word prediction" — it is for **next SENTENCE prediction**. > 6. **Fine-tuning vs. Pre-training confusion** — Fine-tuning uses **labeled data** and a **small LR**; pre-training uses **unlabeled data** and a **large LR**. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **BERT** = **Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers**. - Architecture: **Encoder-only** Transformer (no decoder). - **BERT-Base:** 12 layers, 768 hidden, 12 heads, **110M params**. - **BERT-Large:** 24 layers, 1024 hidden, 16 heads, **340M params**. - **Scaled Dot-Product Attention:** $\text{softmax}(QK^{\top}/\sqrt{d_k})V$ — scaling prevents softmax saturation. - **Multi-Head Attention** = $h$ parallel attention heads, concatenated and projected. - **Input Embedding** = $E_{\text{token}} + E_{\text{segment}} + E_{\text{position}}$. - **Special tokens:** `[CLS]` (classification), `[SEP]` (separator). - **Pre-training Task 1 (MLM):** 15% tokens selected → 80% `[MASK]`, 10% random, 10% unchanged. Predict original via softmax. - **Pre-training Task 2 (NSP):** Binary classification — is sentence B the true next sentence of A? Uses `[CLS]` output. - **Fine-tuning:** Add one task-specific layer; train on labeled data with small LR ($2\text{e-}5$ to $5\text{e-}5$), 2–4 epochs. - **Training Corpus:** Wikipedia + BookCorpus = **3.3 billion words**. - **Max Sequence Length:** **512 tokens**. - **Why bidirectional?** Allows every token to use **both left and right context** → richer semantic understanding. - **Variants:** RoBERTa (no NSP, more data), DistilBERT (smaller, faster), ALBERT (parameter sharing). - **Applications:** Sentiment analysis, QA, NER, NLI, text classification, semantic search. - **Key Difference from GPT:** BERT = **understanding**, GPT = **generation**. - **Bidirectional ≠ BiLSTM:** Both are bidirectional, but BERT uses **self-attention** (parallel, global) vs. BiLSTM (sequential, local). - **No decoder** = BERT cannot naturally generate text; it produces **contextual embeddings** only. - **Layer normalization** and **residual connections** stabilize training in deep stacks. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for BERT pre-training:** **"M & N"** → **M**asked LM + **N**ext Sentence Prediction. Think: **"Berts are MASKed and NSPecting"** 🤓

topics.title: Generative Models: GAN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT525 - ARTIFICIAL NEURAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Generative Models: GAN

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generative Adversarial Network (GAN):** A class of deep generative models introduced by **Ian Goodfellow et al. (2014)** that learns to model the true data distribution $p_{data}(x)$ by setting up a two-player **minimax game** between two neural networks: a **Generator ($G$)** that synthesizes fake samples from a latent noise prior, and a **Discriminator ($D$)** that performs binary classification to distinguish real samples from generated (fake) samples.

Formally, a GAN is defined by a value function $V(D, G)$ that both networks attempt to optimize in opposite directions:

$$\min_{G} \max_{D} V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}(x)}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_{z}(z)}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

The framework falls under **unsupervised learning** and is a foundational pillar of modern generative AI (alongside VAEs, Diffusion Models, and Flow-based models).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Counterfeiter & The Police

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Think of GANs as a high-stakes cat-and-mouse game between a **counterfeiter (Generator)** and a **police detective (Discriminator)**.
>
> - The **Generator ($G$)** is an art forger. Initially, it produces clumsy fake currency from random scribbles (noise vector $z$).
> - The **Discriminator ($D$)** is a sharp-eyed detective trained on a huge pile of real banknotes, learning to shout *"FAKE!"* or *"REAL!"*.
> - The forger $G$ keeps studying the feedback from the detective and improving the fakes.
> - The detective $D$ keeps upgrading its skills, becoming better at spotting ever-more-convincing counterfeits.
> - At **equilibrium (Nash equilibrium)**, the forger's currency is indistinguishable from the real notes, and the detective is reduced to a 50/50 coin flip ($D(x) = 0.5$).
>
> At that point, $G$ has effectively learned $p_{data}(x)$ and can mint infinite realistic samples.

## 1.3 Why GANs Matter in Engineering

- **Synthetic data generation** when real data is scarce or sensitive (medical imaging, fraud detection).
- **Super-resolution, denoising, inpainting** in image processing pipelines.
- **Data augmentation** to boost performance of downstream classifiers.
- **Drug discovery & materials science** — generating novel molecular structures.
- **Simulation of physical systems** when running real experiments is expensive.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Pay special attention to the **minimax objective**, the **alternating training procedure**, **mode collapse**, and the **vanishing gradient problem** — these are the four most-tested concepts in KTU university examinations on this topic.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Components of a GAN

A GAN is composed of two differentiable neural networks, both implemented as **multilayer perceptrons (MLPs)** or **deep convolutional networks (in DCGAN)**:

### 2.1.1 The Generator $G(z; \theta_g)$

- **Input:** A latent noise vector $z \in \mathbb{R}^{n_z}$, sampled from a simple prior distribution $p_z(z)$, typically:
  - **Uniform:** $z \sim \mathcal{U}(-1, 1)$
  - **Gaussian:** $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$
- **Output:** A synthetic sample $G(z) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ living in the data space (e.g., a $28 \times 28$ image flattened to a 784-dim vector).
- **Objective:** Learn a mapping $G: \mathcal{Z} \to \mathcal{X}$ such that the induced distribution $p_g(x)$ closely matches $p_{data}(x)$.

### 2.1.2 The Discriminator $D(x; \theta_d)$

- **Input:** A sample $x$ (either real or generated).
- **Output:** A scalar probability $D(x) \in [0, 1]$ representing the likelihood that $x$ came from $p_{data}$ rather than $p_g$.
- **Objective:** Maximize correct classification: push $D(x) \rightarrow 1$ for real samples, $D(G(z)) \rightarrow 0$ for fake samples.

## 2.2 The Minimax Game — Why "Adversarial"?

The two networks have **opposing** loss landscapes:

| Network | Goal | Loss Direction |
|---|---|---|
| $D$ (Discriminator) | Correctly classify real vs. fake | **Maximize** $V(D, G)$ |
| $G$ (Generator) | Fool the Discriminator | **Minimize** $V(D, G)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The word "Adversarial" reflects this **zero-sum, opposing gradient** relationship. The Discriminator's gain is the Generator's loss, and vice versa.

## 2.3 The Training Algorithm (Alternating Optimization)

GANs are trained via **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** in alternating steps:

1. **Fix $G$, update $D$ for $k$ steps (usually $k = 1$):**
   - Sample a minibatch of $m$ noise samples $\{z^{(1)}, \ldots, z^{(m)}\}$ from $p_z(z)$.
   - Sample a minibatch of $m$ real samples $\{x^{(1)}, \ldots, x^{(m)}\}$ from $p_{data}(x)$.
   - Update $\theta_d$ by **ascending** the stochastic gradient of:
   
   $$\nabla_{\theta_d} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[\log D(x^{(i)}) + \log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)})))\right]$$

2. **Fix $D$, update $G$ for 1 step:**
   - Resample $m$ noise vectors.
   - Update $\theta_g$ by **descending** the stochastic gradient of:
   
   $$\nabla_{\theta_g} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)})))$$

3. **Repeat** for a fixed number of epochs until convergence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Trick (Goodfellow's heuristic):** Early in training, $D$ can easily reject samples because $G$ is poor, causing $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ to saturate (gradient $\approx 0$). To avoid this, in practice, $G$ is trained to **maximize** $\log D(G(z))$ instead of minimizing $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$. Both objectives have the **same fixed point** but provide stronger gradients early on.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Value function | $V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_{z}}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$ | Combined objective |
| Minimax objective | $\min_G \max_D V(D, G)$ | Joint optimization |
| Optimal Discriminator | $D^{*}(x) = \dfrac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$ | Closed-form for fixed $G$ |
| Global optimum | $p_g = p_{data}$ | Nash equilibrium |
| Discriminator at equilibrium | $D^{*}(x) = 0.5$ for all $x$ | Undecidable |
| Value at equilibrium | $V(D^{*}, G^{*}) = -\log 4$ | Minimum achievable |
| Jensen–Shannon Divergence (JSD) | $JS(p_{data} \Vert p_g) = \frac{1}{2} KL(p_{data} \Vert M) + \frac{1}{2} KL(p_g \Vert M)$ | Implicit cost minimized |
| Wasserstein-1 (WGAN) | $W(p_{data}, p_g) = \inf_{\gamma \in \Pi} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \gamma}[\Vert x - y \Vert]$ | Earth-mover distance |
| WGAN critic loss | $L = \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_{z}}[D(G(z))] - \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[D(x)]$ | Lipschitz-constrained |
| Gradient penalty (WGAN-GP) | $\lambda \, \mathbb{E}_{\hat{x}} \left[(\Vert \nabla_{\hat{x}} D(\hat{x}) \Vert_2 - 1)^{2}\right]$ | Enforces 1-Lipschitz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on notation:** We use $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ for norms and $\vert \cdot \vert$ for absolute values throughout this module to avoid visual confusion. The Jensen–Shannon Divergence (JSD) is the **implicit cost function** that a standard GAN minimizes when trained to optimality — a fact that will be derived in Section 3.

## 2.5 Failure Modes of Vanilla GANs

### 2.5.1 Mode Collapse

> [!WARNING]
> The Generator collapses to producing a **few highly similar samples** (or even a single sample) that consistently fool $D$. The model essentially ignores the diversity of $p_{data}$ and "gives up" exploring the latent space.

**Cause:** Generator finds a few "safe" outputs that exploit weaknesses in $D$.

### 2.5.2 Vanishing Gradients

When $D$ is too strong, $D(G(z)) \approx 0$ for all $z$, so $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ saturates near 0 and provides **almost no gradient** to update $G$.

### 2.5.3 Training Instability

The min-max formulation is **not a convex-concave** problem in general — the parameter space is non-convex, and high-dimensional landscapes contain many saddle points. Convergence to a Nash equilibrium is **not guaranteed**.

## 2.6 Variants of GANs (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Variant | Year | Key Innovation |
|---|---|---|
| **DCGAN** | 2015 | Replaces MLPs with **deep convolutional** $G$ and $D$; uses batch norm, ReLU/LeakyReLU |
| **Conditional GAN (cGAN)** | 2014 | Conditions both $G$ and $D$ on a label $y$ for class-specific generation |
| **Wasserstein GAN (WGAN)** | 2017 | Replaces JSD with **Earth-Mover (Wasserstein-1) distance**; uses weight clipping |
| **WGAN-GP** | 2017 | Replaces weight clipping with **gradient penalty** for stable 1-Lipschitz constraint |
| **InfoGAN** | 2016 | Learns **disentangled latent codes** via mutual information maximization |
| **CycleGAN** | 2017 | Unpaired image-to-image translation using **cycle-consistency** loss |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare:** Synthesizing chest X-rays to augment scarce COVID-19 datasets.
- **Automotive:** Generating synthetic driving scenarios for autonomous vehicle training.
- **Manufacturing:** Simulating defect textures for quality-control CNNs.
- **Entertainment:** Face aging, deepfake detection research, virtual avatars.
- **Telecommunications:** Channel modeling for 5G/6G simulation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Optimal Discriminator $D^{*}(x)$

We are given a fixed Generator $G$, with induced distribution $p_g$. We want to find the Discriminator $D$ that maximizes:

$$V(D, G) = \int_{x} p_{data}(x) \log(D(x)) \, dx + \int_{z} p_{z}(z) \log(1 - D(G(z))) \, dz$$

**Step 1 — Change of variables in the second integral.**

By the change-of-variable rule for probability densities, sampling $z \sim p_z$ and computing $G(z)$ induces the distribution $p_g$. So the second term can be rewritten as:

$$V(D, G) = \int_{x} p_{data}(x) \log D(x) \, dx + \int_{x} p_{g}(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \, dx$$

**Step 2 — Combine into a single integral.**

$$V(D, G) = \int_{x} \Big[ p_{data}(x) \log D(x) + p_{g}(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \Big] \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Pointwise optimization.**

For any $x$ where both $p_{data}(x) > 0$ and $p_g(x) > 0$, the integrand is a function of the scalar $D(x) \in [0, 1]$. We can maximize it independently at every point $x$ by differentiating with respect to $D(x)$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial D(x)} \Big[ p_{data}(x) \log D(x) + p_{g}(x) \log(1 - D(x)) \Big] = 0$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate the log terms.**

$$\frac{p_{data}(x)}{D(x)} - \frac{p_{g}(x)}{1 - D(x)} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $D(x)$.**

Multiply both sides by $D(x)(1 - D(x))$:

$$p_{data}(x)\,(1 - D(x)) = p_g(x)\, D(x)$$

$$p_{data}(x) - p_{data}(x) D(x) = p_g(x) D(x)$$

$$p_{data}(x) = D(x)\bigl(p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)\bigr)$$

$$\boxed{D^{*}(x) = \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_{g}(x)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **optimal discriminator** for any fixed generator. It is the ratio of the real density to the total (real + generated) density. Note that $0 \le D^{*}(x) \le 1$, as required for a probability.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Global Optimum $p_g = p_{data}$

We now substitute $D^{*}(x)$ back into $V(D, G)$ to find the optimal $G$.

**Step 1 — Write the value function at $D^{*}$.**

$$V(D^{*}, G) = \int_{x} p_{data}(x) \log \frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)} \, dx + \int_{x} p_g(x) \log \frac{p_g(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)} \, dx$$

**Step 2 — Introduce a notational shorthand.**

Let $M(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)\bigr)$ be the average of the two distributions. Then $p_{data}(x) + p_g(x) = 2M(x)$, and the two log arguments become $\frac{p_{data}(x)}{2M(x)}$ and $\frac{p_g(x)}{2M(x)}$.

**Step 3 — Pull out constants.**

$$V(D^{*}, G) = \int_{x} p_{data}(x) \Big[ \log p_{data}(x) - \log 2 - \log M(x) \Big] dx + \int_{x} p_g(x) \Big[ \log p_g(x) - \log 2 - \log M(x) \Big] dx$$

**Step 4 — Split the integrals and use the KL definition.**

Recall the Kullback–Leibler divergence: $KL(P \Vert Q) = \int P \log \frac{P}{Q}$. We can therefore group:

$$V(D^{*}, G) = -\log 4 + KL\!\left(p_{data} \Vert M\right) + KL\!\left(p_{g} \Vert M\right)$$

where the $-\log 4$ comes from the two $-\log 2$ terms integrated over the probability mass (each integrates to 1).

**Step 5 — Express in terms of JSD.**

By definition, the **Jensen–Shannon Divergence** is:

$$JS(p_{data} \Vert p_{g}) = \tfrac{1}{2} KL(p_{data} \Vert M) + \tfrac{1}{2} KL(p_{g} \Vert M)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{V(D^{*}, G) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot JS(p_{data} \Vert p_{g})}$$

**Step 6 — Minimize over $G$.**

Since the JSD is **non-negative** and equals **zero if and only if** $p_{data} = p_g$ (up to a set of measure zero), the value function is minimized uniquely when:

$$p_g^{*} = p_{data}$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the global minimum.**

At the global optimum:

$$\boxed{V(D^{*}, G^{*}) = -\log 4 \approx -1.3863}$$

and the optimal discriminator becomes $D^{*}(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}$ everywhere — a purely random classifier, as expected.

## 3.3 Gradient Updates on the Generator (Alternative Form)

In practice, $G$ is updated by **ascending** $\log D(G(z))$ rather than descending $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$. Let us confirm these have the same fixed point.

Compute the gradient of $G$'s loss using the chain rule on a single sample $z$:

$$\nabla_{\theta_g} \, \log(1 - D(G(z))) = \frac{-1}{1 - D(G(z))} \cdot \nabla_{\theta_g} D(G(z)) = \frac{-1}{1 - D(G(z))} \cdot D'(G(z)) \cdot \nabla_{\theta_g} G(z)$$

At the **fixed point** where $D(G(z)) \to 0$ (i.e., $D$ perfectly rejects all fakes), this gradient vanishes, which is precisely the **vanishing gradient problem**.

For the **non-saturating** version, maximize $\log D(G(z))$:

$$\nabla_{\theta_g} \, \log D(G(z)) = \frac{1}{D(G(z))} \cdot D'(G(z)) \cdot \nabla_{\theta_g} G(z)$$

When $D(G(z)) \to 0$, this gradient **blows up**, providing a strong learning signal to $G$ exactly when it is most needed. The two objectives share the same fixed point $D(G(z)) = 0.5$, but the non-saturating form avoids early-training saturation.

## 3.4 WGAN Value Function (for comparison)

To fix the vanishing-gradient pathology, Arjovsky et al. (2017) proposed replacing the JSD-based objective with the **Wasserstein-1 distance**:

$$\min_{G} \max_{D \in \mathcal{L}_1} \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[D(x)] - \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[D(G(z))]$$

where $\mathcal{L}_1$ is the set of all 1-Lipschitz functions. The key advantage is that the Wasserstein distance is **continuous and differentiable almost everywhere**, even when $p_g$ and $p_{data}$ have disjoint supports — which is exactly the regime in which standard GANs fail.

```mermaid

    A0[Start: Initialize G and D weights] --> A1[Step 1: Sample minibatch of real x from pdata]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Sample minibatch of noise z from pz]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Generate fakes Gz]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Update D by ASCENDING gradient of V]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Resample noise z]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Update G by DESCENDING G-loss or ASCENDING non-saturating loss]
    A6 --> A7{Convergence or max epochs?}

    A7 -- Yes --> A8[End: Use G to sample from learned pg]

    class A7 decision
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q1. Define a Generative Adversarial Network. Mention the role of the Generator and the Discriminator.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Generative Adversarial Network (GAN), introduced by Ian Goodfellow et al. in 2014, is a class of deep generative models that learns the data distribution $p_{data}$ by framing training as a two-player **minimax game**.

- **Generator ($G$):** A neural network that maps a random noise vector $z \sim p_z$ to a synthetic data sample $G(z)$, trying to mimic real data.
- **Discriminator ($D$):** A binary classifier that outputs the probability $D(x)$ that a given input $x$ is real (sourced from $p_{data}$) rather than fake (produced by $G$).

The two networks are trained **adversarially** — $D$ tries to maximize classification accuracy, while $G$ tries to minimize $D$'s ability to do so. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for $G$, 1 for $D$.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q2. What is mode collapse in GANs? State any one remedy.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Mode collapse** is a failure mode of GANs in which the Generator learns to produce only a **small subset of the data distribution** (sometimes even a single repeated sample), ignoring the diversity of $p_{data}$. The Generator essentially exploits a blind spot in the Discriminator.

**Remedy (any one of the following):**
- Use **minibatch discrimination** — let $D$ compare samples within a batch to detect lack of diversity.
- Use **unrolled GANs** — backpropagate through $D$'s future updates to prevent $G$ from overfitting to $D$.
- Switch to a **Wasserstein GAN (WGAN)**, which encourages diversity via the Earth-Mover distance.
- Use **Spectral Normalization** to stabilize $D$ and prevent it from over-specializing.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for remedy.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module-Level Question — Internal Choice: Attempt any ONE of the following.**

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and training procedure of a Generative Adversarial Network. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The GAN architecture consists of two neural networks trained in opposition:

1. **Generator Network $G$:** Takes as input a noise vector $z$ sampled from a prior $p_z(z)$ (typically Gaussian or uniform) and produces a synthetic sample $G(z)$. It is implemented as an MLP or (in DCGAN) a deep transposed-convolutional network.

2. **Discriminator Network $D$:** Takes as input either a real sample $x \sim p_{data}$ or a fake sample $G(z)$, and outputs a probability $D(x) \in [0, 1]$ indicating "real" or "fake". It is a standard binary classifier.

**Training Procedure (alternating SGD):**

- **Step 1 (Train $D$):** Sample a minibatch of $m$ real examples and $m$ noise vectors. Update $D$ by **ascending** its loss so it better distinguishes real from fake.
- **Step 2 (Train $G$):** Resample $m$ noise vectors. Update $G$ by **descending** its loss (or ascending the non-saturating surrogate $\log D(G(z))$) so it produces more convincing fakes.
- **Step 3:** Repeat the two steps alternately for a number of epochs.

At convergence, the system reaches a **Nash equilibrium** where $p_g = p_{data}$ and $D$ outputs $0.5$ for every input.

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram description, 3 for roles of $G$ and $D$, 2 for the alternating training loop.)*

---

**Q. (b)** Derive the optimal discriminator $D^{*}(x)$ for a fixed generator and show that the global optimum of the GAN objective is achieved when $p_g = p_{data}$, with $V(D^{*}, G^{*}) = -\log 4$. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:** *(See Section 3.1 and 3.2 of these notes for the full derivation. The key steps expected are:)*

1. Write the value function as a single integral over $x$ using the change-of-variable $G(z) \to x$ with density $p_g(x)$.
2. Differentiate the integrand with respect to $D(x)$ and set to zero to obtain $D^{*}(x) = \dfrac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$.
3. Substitute $D^{*}$ back into $V(D, G)$ and rewrite using the average distribution $M(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}(p_{data}(x) + p_g(x))$.
4. Recognize the resulting expression as $V(D^{*}, G) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot JS(p_{data} \Vert p_g)$.
5. Argue that the JSD is minimized (equals 0) if and only if $p_g = p_{data}$, giving $V(D^{*}, G^{*}) = -\log 4$.

At the optimum, $D^{*}(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}$ for all $x$, meaning the discriminator can no longer tell real from fake.

*(7 marks: 3 for deriving $D^{*}$, 2 for expressing the value at $D^{*}$, 2 for the global optimum and its value.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the vanishing gradient problem in GANs. How does the Wasserstein GAN (WGAN) address it? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**The Vanishing Gradient Problem:**

In the standard GAN, the Generator's loss is $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$. Early in training, the Discriminator easily rejects fake samples, so $D(G(z)) \approx 0$, which means $\log(1 - D(G(z))) \approx 0$. The gradient of this saturating function is nearly zero:

$$\frac{d}{dD}\log(1 - D)\Big|_{D \to 0} = \frac{-1}{1 - D}\Big|_{D \to 0} = -1 \quad \text{(small finite)}$$

More critically, when $D$ is near-optimal, it provides **almost no useful learning signal** to $G$, because the JSD between $p_{data}$ and $p_g$ becomes **saturated at $\log 2$** whenever the supports are disjoint. The gradient then vanishes in the high-dimensional parameter space, halting $G$'s learning.

**How WGAN Fixes It:**

WGAN replaces the JSD-based objective with the **Wasserstein-1 (Earth-Mover) distance**:

$$W(p_{data}, p_g) = \inf_{\gamma \in \Pi(p_{data}, p_g)} \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \gamma}[\Vert x - y \Vert]$$

Crucially, $W$ is **continuous and differentiable almost everywhere**, even when the two distributions have **disjoint supports**. This means the gradient of $W$ with respect to $G$'s parameters is **never zero**, providing a stable learning signal throughout training. The Discriminator (now called the **Critic**) is constrained to be 1-Lipschitz via **weight clipping** (original WGAN) or **gradient penalty** (WGAN-GP).

*(7 marks: 3 for explaining vanishing gradient, 2 for JSD saturation, 2 for WGAN's Wasserstein solution.)*

---

**Q. (b)** Compare Vanilla GAN, DCGAN, Conditional GAN, and WGAN with respect to architecture, loss function, advantages, and limitations. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Vanilla GAN | DCGAN | Conditional GAN (cGAN) | WGAN |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | MLPs for $G$ and $D$ | Deep ConvNets (transposed conv in $G$, strided conv in $D$) | $G$ and $D$ receive additional label $y$ | Critic $D$ (no sigmoid output) |
| **Loss function** | JSD-based minimax $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ | Same as Vanilla | Same as Vanilla, conditioned on $y$ | Wasserstein-1 distance |
| **Key innovation** | Foundational min-max setup | Stable training via batch-norm, ReLU/LeakyReLU | Class-conditional generation | Earth-mover distance; smooth gradients |
| **Advantages** | Conceptually simple, theoretically elegant | Better image fidelity, more stable | Allows controlled generation | No vanishing gradient; meaningful loss curve |
| **Limitations** | Vanishing gradient, mode collapse, unstable | Still suffers from mode collapse | Needs labelled data | Weight clipping can be too restrictive; needs Lipschitz enforcement |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Don't** confuse the **Critic** in WGAN with the **Discriminator** in standard GAN. The Critic outputs an **unbounded real number**, not a probability in $[0, 1]$ — and that is by design.
> - **Don't** claim WGAN solves mode collapse completely. It mitigates vanishing gradients and improves stability, but mode collapse can still occur.
> - **Don't** state that the JSD is differentiable everywhere. It is **constant** ($\log 2$) on disjoint supports, which is precisely why gradients vanish.
> - **Always** mention the role of the **1-Lipschitz constraint** in WGAN when comparing it to vanilla GAN.

*(7 marks: 4 for the comparison table content, 1 for architectural distinction, 1 for loss function difference, 1 for advantages/limitations contrast.)*

---
