> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam on GANs**

- [x] GAN stands for **Generative Adversarial Network** — proposed by **Ian Goodfellow (2014)**.
- [x] Two networks: **Generator $G$ (forger)** and **Discriminator $D$ (detective)**.
- [x] Objective is a **minimax game**: $\min_G \max_D V(D, G)$.
- [x] Value function: $V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_{z}}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$.
- [x] **Optimal Discriminator**: $D^{*}(x) = \dfrac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x) + p_g(x)}$.
- [x] **Global optimum**: $p_g = p_{data}$, $D^{*}(x) = 0.5$, $V = -\log 4$.
- [x] GAN implicitly minimizes **Jensen–Shannon Divergence (JSD)**.
- [x] **Non-saturating trick**: $G$ maximizes $\log D(G(z))$ instead of minimizing $\log(1 - D(G(z)))$ to avoid early-training saturation.
- [x] **Mode collapse**: Generator produces limited variety of samples.
- [x] **Vanishing gradient**: Occurs when $D$ becomes too strong; JSD saturates.
- [x] **WGAN**: Replaces JSD with Wasserstein-1 distance; uses Critic + weight clipping / gradient penalty.
- [x] **DCGAN**: Uses deep convolutional layers for $G$ and $D$ with batch normalization.
- [x] **Conditional GAN**: Adds label $y$ to both $G$ and $D$ for class-specific generation.
- [x] Training is **alternating SGD**: $k$ steps of $D$, then 1 step of $G$ (typically $k = 1$).
- [x] Equilibrium is a **Nash equilibrium** — convergence is **not guaranteed** in general.
- [x] Practical evaluation metrics: **Inception Score (IS)**, **Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications in Biomedical Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT525 - ARTIFICIAL NEURAL NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications in Biomedical Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomedical Engineering Applications of Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs)** refer to the systematic deployment of biologically-inspired computational models—comprising interconnected processing units (neurons) organized into input, hidden, and output layers—for the automated analysis, interpretation, classification, and prediction of complex biological and medical data. These applications span **medical imaging**, **physiological signal processing** (ECG, EEG, EMG), **clinical decision support**, **drug discovery**, and **rehabilitation engineering**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, biomedical applications of ANNs leverage the universal approximation theorem and pattern-recognition capability of **Multilayer Perceptrons (MLP)**, **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)**, **Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN)**, and hybrid deep-learning architectures to process high-dimensional, non-linear, and noisy medical datasets.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Radiology Resident:**
> Imagine training a junior doctor to detect tumors in MRI scans. Initially, the resident makes mistakes. But after being shown thousands of labeled scans (benign vs. malignant) and receiving corrective feedback for every wrong prediction, the resident develops an intuitive "sixth sense" for spotting subtle patterns invisible to untrained eyes. ANNs work identically — they are **algorithmic residents** that learn diagnostic patterns from vast datasets of patient records, images, and signals, eventually outperforming rule-based systems in detecting pathologies.

## 1.3 Why Neural Networks Excel in Biomedical Engineering

| Biomedical Challenge | Why ANNs are Suited |
|---|---|
| **High-dimensional data** (e.g., 3D MRI volumes) | ANNs perform automatic **feature extraction** without hand-crafted rules |
| **Non-linear physiological relationships** | Activation functions (ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh) model complex mappings |
| **Noisy biosignals** (ECG artifacts, motion noise) | Robustness to noise via regularization and dropout |
| **Small labelled datasets** | Transfer learning & data augmentation via GANs |
| **Real-time inference** (ICU monitoring) | GPU-accelerated parallel computation |

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics in Biomedical ANN (Bolded Constants)

> [!NOTE]
> The following **key performance indicators** are universally adopted in KTU-aligned biomedical ANN literature:
> - **Sensitivity (Recall)**: $S_e = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ — fraction of true positives correctly identified
> - **Specificity**: $S_p = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ — fraction of true negatives correctly rejected
> - **Accuracy**: $Acc = \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$
> - **Dice Coefficient (F1-Score)**: $DSC = \dfrac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ — segmentation overlap measure
> - **AUC-ROC**: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Taxonomy for Biomedical Tasks

### 2.1.1 Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) — Medical Imaging
CNNs are the **de-facto standard** for analyzing radiology images. The convolution operation automatically learns spatial hierarchies — edges → textures → organs → pathologies.

**Why CNNs work for biomedical imaging:**
- **Local connectivity**: Each neuron examines a small receptive field
- **Weight sharing**: Same filter detects the same feature anywhere in the image
- **Translation invariance**: A tumor in the upper-left is detected equally as in the lower-right

**Popular CNN variants in biomedical context:**
- **U-Net**: For medical image segmentation (Ronneberger, 2015)
- **ResNet / DenseNet**: For classification of X-rays and histopathology
- **VGG-16 / Inception**: Transfer learning base models

### 2.1.2 Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) & LSTMs — Biosignal Analysis
Physiological signals (ECG, EEG, EMG) are inherently **temporal sequences**. RNNs maintain a hidden state that captures temporal dependencies.

- **Vanilla RNN**: Suffers from vanishing gradients
- **LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)**: Uses gating mechanisms to retain long-term dependencies
- **GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)**: Simplified LSTM with fewer parameters

**Key LSTM Equations:**

$$f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$$

$$i_t = \sigma(W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i)$$

$$\tilde{C}_t = \tanh(W_C \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_C)$$

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

$$o_t = \sigma(W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o)$$

$$h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)$$

> where $\sigma$ is the sigmoid function, $\odot$ denotes element-wise multiplication, $f_t, i_t, o_t$ are forget, input, and output gates, and $C_t$ is the cell state.

### 2.1.3 Autoencoders — Anomaly Detection & Denoising
Autoencoders compress medical data into a latent representation and reconstruct it. **Reconstruction error** flags anomalies (e.g., tumors, arrhythmias).

$$\mathcal{L}_{AE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert x_i - \hat{x}_i \Vert_2^2$$

### 2.1.4 Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) — Data Augmentation
GANs synthesize realistic medical images to overcome data scarcity in rare-disease training sets.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates every high-yield equation tested in KTU 2024 ESE for Module 4.

| Concept | Equation | Variables / Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Neuron output | $y = \varphi\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b\right)$ | $w_i$: weights, $b$: bias, $\varphi$: activation |
| Sigmoid activation | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Output range $(0,1)$ |
| ReLU activation | $\varphi(z) = \max(0, z)$ | Mitigates vanishing gradient |
| Softmax (multi-class) | $\sigma(z_j) = \dfrac{e^{z_j}}{\sum_{k=1}^{K} e^{z_k}}$ | Probabilistic class output |
| Binary Cross-Entropy | $\mathcal{L}_{BCE} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y_i\log\hat{y}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\right]$ | For binary diagnosis (e.g., tumor/no tumor) |
| Categorical Cross-Entropy | $\mathcal{L}_{CCE} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c}\log\hat{y}_{i,c}$ | For multi-class disease classification |
| Dice Loss (Segmentation) | $\mathcal{L}_{Dice} = 1 - \dfrac{2\sum p_i g_i + \epsilon}{\sum p_i + \sum g_i + \epsilon}$ | $p_i$: prediction, $g_i$: ground truth |
| Convolution Operation | $S(i,j) = (K * X)(i,j) = \sum_{m}\sum_{n} K(m,n) \cdot X(i-m, j-n)$ | $K$: kernel, $X$: input feature map |
| Max Pooling | $y_{i,j,c} = \max_{(p,q) \in \mathcal{R}_{i,j}} x_{p,q,c}$ | $\mathcal{R}_{i,j}$: pooling region |
| Backpropagation Update | $w_{ij}^{(l)} \leftarrow w_{ij}^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_{ij}^{(l)}}$ | $\eta$: learning rate |
| LSTM Forget Gate | $f_t = \sigma(W_f [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$ | Controls memory retention |
| Sensitivity (Recall) | $S_e = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | True positive rate |
| Specificity | $S_p = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | True negative rate |
| Dice Similarity Coefficient | $DSC = \dfrac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | Segmentation overlap |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern clinical practice, ANNs:
- **Reduce diagnostic time** from 30 minutes to under 5 seconds for chest X-ray triage
- **Enable remote diagnostics** via cloud-deployed models on edge devices
- **Power assistive devices** such as neural prosthetics decoding EEG signals
- **Accelerate pharmaceutical R&D** by predicting drug-target interactions
- **Support personalized medicine** by predicting patient-specific treatment responses

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Gradient of Binary Cross-Entropy Loss with Sigmoid Output (Used in Tumor/No-Tumor Classification)

This is a **mandatory KTU derivation** because it ties together (a) the activation function, (b) the loss function, and (c) backpropagation—all tested frequently in KTU ESE.

### Step 1 — Define the Network Output
A single-output neuron produces a probability via the **sigmoid** activation:

$$\hat{y} = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

where the pre-activation $z$ is:

$$z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$$

### Step 2 — State the Loss Function
For binary medical classification (e.g., malignant vs. benign), the **Binary Cross-Entropy** loss over $N$ samples is:

$$\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i) \right]$$

For a single sample (dropping the index for clarity):

$$\mathcal{L} = -\left[ y \log(\hat{y}) + (1 - y) \log(1 - \hat{y}) \right]$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate Sigmoid (A Crucial Identity)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sigmoid derivative identity:** $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z) \cdot [1 - \sigma(z)] = \hat{y}(1-\hat{y})$

Derivation:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = \frac{d}{dz}\left(1 + e^{-z}\right)^{-1} = -\left(1 + e^{-z}\right)^{-2} \cdot \left(-e^{-z}\right)$$

$$= \frac{e^{-z}}{(1+e^{-z})^2} = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}} \cdot \frac{e^{-z}}{1+e^{-z}}$$

$$= \sigma(z) \cdot [1 - \sigma(z)] = \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})$$

### Step 4 — Compute $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z}$ (Gradient w.r.t. Pre-activation)

Apply the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z}$$

First term:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{y}} = -\left[\frac{y}{\hat{y}} - \frac{1-y}{1-\hat{y}}\right] = \frac{\hat{y} - y}{\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})}$$

Second term (from Step 3):

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z} = \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y})$$

Multiplying:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} = \frac{\hat{y} - y}{\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})} \cdot \hat{y}(1-\hat{y}) = \hat{y} - y$$

> **Beautiful result:** The gradient of BCE w.r.t. pre-activation is simply the **prediction error** $\hat{y} - y$.

### Step 5 — Compute $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_i}$ and $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b}$

Using chain rule again, and noting that $\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial w_i} = x_i$ and $\dfrac{\partial z}{\partial b} = 1$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_i} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial w_i} = (\hat{y} - y) \cdot x_i$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial b} = \hat{y} - y$$

### Step 6 — Apply Gradient Descent Update Rule

$$w_i \leftarrow w_i - \eta \cdot (\hat{y} - y) \cdot x_i$$

$$b \leftarrow b - \eta \cdot (\hat{y} - y)$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** (typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ for biomedical deep learning).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The weight update is proportional to the **prediction error** and the **input feature**. This is why BCE + Sigmoid is the canonical pairing for medical binary diagnosis in KTU examinations.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: ECG Beat Classification Using a 1-D CNN

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Style):**
A single-lead ECG recording of length 360 samples (1 beat) is fed to a 1-D CNN to classify it as **Normal (0)** or **Arrhythmic (1)**. The network has:
- Input: $x \in \mathbb{R}^{1 \times 360}$
- Conv1D layer: 8 filters, kernel size 5, stride 1, ReLU
- MaxPool1D: pool size 2
- Flatten → Dense(1) → Sigmoid

Given an input window centered at a QRS complex with the values shown, compute the output of the first conv filter (assume filter weights = $[+1, +1, 0, -1, -1]$ — a simplified QRS detector — and bias $= 0$). Assume the first 5 input samples are $[0.1, 0.2, 0.8, 0.3, 0.1]$.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
z_1 &= \sum_{k=1}^{5} w_k \cdot x_k + b \\
&= (+1)(0.1) + (+1)(0.2) + (0)(0.8) + (-1)(0.3) + (-1)(0.1) + 0 \\
&= 0.1 + 0.2 + 0 - 0.3 - 0.1 \\
&= -0.1
\end{aligned}$$

Apply ReLU:

$$a_1 = \max(0, -0.1) = 0$$

This filter responded weakly — the network learns the appropriate filter weights through training to maximize classification accuracy.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Biomedical ANN Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Biomedical Data] --> B[Pre-processing Module]
    B --> C[Feature Extraction Layer]
    C --> D[Neural Network Core]
    D --> E[Output Decision Layer]
    E --> F[Clinical Interpretation]
    F --> G[Feedback to Clinician]
    G --> H[Model Retraining Loop]
    H --> D
    A --> A1[ECG / EEG / EMG Signals]
    A --> A2[CT / MRI / X-ray Images]
    A --> A3[Genomic Sequences]
    A1 --> B
    A2 --> B
    A3 --> B
    B --> B1[Noise Filtering]
    B --> B2[Normalization]
    B --> B3[Segmentation]
    B1 --> C
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[CNN for Images]
    C --> C2[LSTM for Time Series]
    C --> C3[Autoencoder for Anomaly]
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1[Dense Layers]
    D --> D2[Dropout Regularization]
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    E --> E1[Softmax Classifier]
    E --> E2[Sigmoid for Binary]
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    F --> F1[Disease Diagnosis]
    F --> F2[Tumor Localization]
    F --> F3[Arrhythmia Detection]
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The flowchart captures the **universal ANN-in-biomedicine pipeline** that KTU expects students to recognize. Inputs branch into three modalities (signals, images, sequences), each routed through modality-specific feature extractors before converging at a shared neural network core.

## 4.2 CNN Architecture for Medical Image Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP[Input Image 224x224x3] --> C1[Conv2D 32 filters 3x3 ReLU]
    C1 --> P1[MaxPool 2x2]
    P1 --> C2[Conv2D 64 filters 3x3 ReLU]
    C2 --> P2[MaxPool 2x2]
    P2 --> C3[Conv2D 128 filters 3x3 ReLU]
    C3 --> P3[MaxPool 2x2]
    P3 --> FL[Flatten Layer]
    FL --> FC1[Dense 256 ReLU]
    FC1 --> DR[Dropout 0.5]
    DR --> FC2[Dense 2 Softmax]
    FC2 --> OUT[Output Class Probabilities]
```

## 4.3 LSTM Cell for ECG Sequence Processing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    XT[Input x_t] --> FG[Forget Gate f_t]
    XT --> IG[Input Gate i_t]
    XT --> CS[Candidate Cell State tilde C_t]
    HX[Previous h_t-1] --> FG
    HX --> IG
    HX --> CS
    FG --> MUX1[Memory Mixing]
    CP[Previous Cell C_t-1] --> MUX1
    IG --> MUX2[New Info Gate]
    CS --> MUX2
    MUX1 --> CURR[New Cell State C_t]
    MUX2 --> CURR
    CURR --> OT[Output Gate o_t]
    CURR --> TAH[tanh Activation]
    OT --> MUL[Element-wise Multiply]
    TAH --> MUL
    MUL --> HT[New Hidden State h_t]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Node Safety Note:** All node identifiers in the above diagrams use alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `INP`, `C1`, `FC1`, `MUX1`) in compliance with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 rendering safeguards. No reserved keywords are used as standalone node IDs.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*List and briefly explain any three major applications of Artificial Neural Networks in biomedical engineering.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Medical Image Classification (1 mark):** CNNs like ResNet and DenseNet are used to classify X-rays, CT, and MRI images for diseases such as pneumonia, tuberculosis, and lung cancer. They learn hierarchical features automatically, eliminating manual feature engineering.

2. **ECG/EEG Signal Analysis (1 mark):** RNNs and LSTMs classify cardiac arrhythmias from ECG signals and detect epileptic seizures from EEG recordings. Their temporal memory captures beat-to-beat and waveform dependencies essential for diagnosis.

3. **Medical Image Segmentation (1 mark):** U-Net architecture segments tumors, organs, and lesions from radiology images, pixel-by-pixel. It uses an encoder–decoder structure with skip connections and is the gold standard for biomedical segmentation.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Define the Dice Similarity Coefficient and state its significance in evaluating medical image segmentation models.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) measures the overlap between the predicted segmentation mask $A$ and the ground-truth mask $B$:

$$DSC = \frac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$$

where the numerator is twice the cardinality of the intersection, and the denominator is the sum of the cardinalities of both sets.

**Significance (1 mark):** DSC ranges from **0 (no overlap)** to **1 (perfect overlap)**. In medical imaging, a DSC $\geq 0.7$ is generally considered a good segmentation, and $\geq 0.9$ is excellent. It is preferred over pixel accuracy because medical images are highly imbalanced (tumor occupies very few pixels versus background).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students answer **one of two alternatives** in Part B. Both questions below carry 7 + 7 sub-parts.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the general architecture of a **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** used for medical image classification. Explain the role of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hospital deploys a CNN-based system to detect pneumonia from chest X-rays. The confusion matrix on a test set of 200 patients is as follows:

| | Predicted Pneumonia | Predicted Normal |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Pneumonia** | TP = 80 | FN = 20 |
| **Actual Normal** | FP = 10 | TN = 90 |

Calculate the **Accuracy**, **Sensitivity (Recall)**, **Specificity**, and **Precision** of the system. Comment on whether the system is suitable for clinical deployment. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) CNN Architecture for Medical Image Classification (7 marks):**

The CNN architecture consists of the following sequential layers:

1. **Input Layer (1 mark):** Accepts the medical image (e.g., chest X-ray of size $224 \times 224 \times 3$). Pixel values are normalized to $[0,1]$.

2. **Convolutional Layer (2 marks):** Applies learnable filters (kernels) that slide across the image performing the convolution operation:

$$S(i,j) = (K * X)(i,j) = \sum_{m}\sum_{n} K(m,n) \cdot X(i-m, j-n)$$

Each filter extracts a specific feature (edges, textures, blob-like structures). Multiple filters produce multiple feature maps. ReLU activation introduces non-linearity:

$$\varphi(z) = \max(0, z)$$

3. **Pooling Layer (1 mark):** Max-pooling downsamples feature maps, reducing computation and providing translation invariance:

$$y_{i,j,c} = \max_{(p,q) \in \mathcal{R}_{i,j}} x_{p,q,c}$$

4. **Fully Connected (Dense) Layer (1 mark):** After several conv-pool blocks, the feature maps are flattened and fed to dense layers that perform high-level reasoning.

5. **Output Layer (1 mark):** A softmax or sigmoid activation produces class probabilities (e.g., Pneumonia / Normal). Cross-entropy loss is used for training.

6. **Regularization (1 mark):** Dropout layers randomly deactivate neurons during training to prevent overfitting — critical for medical datasets that are often small.

**(b) Performance Metrics Computation (7 marks):**

Given: $TP = 80$, $FN = 20$, $FP = 10$, $TN = 90$, $N = 200$.

**Accuracy (2 marks):**

$$Acc = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{80 + 90}{200} = \frac{170}{200} = 0.85 \text{ or } 85\%$$

**Sensitivity / Recall (2 marks):**

$$S_e = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80 \text{ or } 80\%$$

**Specificity (1.5 marks):**

$$S_p = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{90}{90 + 10} = \frac{90}{100} = 0.90 \text{ or } 90\%$$

**Precision (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{80}{80 + 10} = \frac{80}{90} \approx 0.8889 \text{ or } 88.89\%$$

**Clinical Deployment Comment:**
- Sensitivity of **80%** means 20% of actual pneumonia cases are **missed** — clinically dangerous.
- For screening tools, sensitivity is **more critical** than accuracy because missed diagnoses (false negatives) delay life-saving treatment.
- The system should be used as a **decision-support tool**, not a stand-alone diagnostic, until sensitivity exceeds 95%.

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the use of **LSTM networks** for ECG-based arrhythmia detection. Derive the role of the **forget gate** with its mathematical formulation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A research group uses an **autoencoder** for anomaly detection in brain MRI scans. The autoencoder is trained only on healthy brain MRIs. During testing, the reconstruction errors (in arbitrary units) for 5 healthy and 5 tumor-affected scans are:

| Scan # | Healthy Error | Tumor Error |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.02 | 0.45 |
| 2 | 0.05 | 0.62 |
| 3 | 0.03 | 0.38 |
| 4 | 0.04 | 0.55 |
| 5 | 0.03 | 0.50 |

Compute the mean reconstruction error for each class. Choose a threshold $T$ and justify it. If a new MRI yields a reconstruction error of **0.35**, classify it as healthy or tumor-affected. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) LSTM for ECG Arrhythmia Detection (7 marks):**

**Background (2 marks):** ECG signals are sequential time-series data. Traditional feedforward networks cannot capture long-term temporal dependencies such as PR intervals, QT variability, and beat-to-beat rhythm. **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** networks, a special variant of RNNs, address this by introducing a memory cell $C_t$ regulated by three gates.

**Forget Gate Derivation (3 marks):** The forget gate $f_t$ decides what information to discard from the previous cell state $C_{t-1}$:

$$f_t = \sigma(W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f)$$

where:
- $x_t$ is the current ECG sample (input)
- $h_{t-1}$ is the previous hidden state
- $W_f$ is the weight matrix, $b_f$ the bias
- $\sigma$ is the sigmoid function, outputting values in $[0, 1]$
- $f_t \to 0$: **forget** old information (e.g., irrelevant baseline wander)
- $f_t \to 1$: **retain** all information (e.g., QRS complex pattern)

The cell state is updated as:

$$C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{C}_t$$

**Application to Arrhythmia (2 marks):** In arrhythmia detection, LSTMs process sliding windows of ECG samples. The forget gate learns to retain morphological features of normal QRS complexes and discard noise. Deviations (e.g., wide QRS in ventricular tachycardia) cause the hidden state trajectory to diverge, triggering the classifier to output "arrhythmic." Models like **DeepHeart** and **CardioNet** have achieved > 95% accuracy on the MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database.

**(b) Autoencoder Anomaly Detection (7 marks):**

**Mean Reconstruction Errors (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{E}_{\text{healthy}} &= \frac{0.02 + 0.05 + 0.03 + 0.04 + 0.03}{5} = \frac{0.17}{5} = 0.034 \\
\bar{E}_{\text{tumor}} &= \frac{0.45 + 0.62 + 0.38 + 0.55 + 0.50}{5} = \frac{2.50}{5} = 0.500
\end{aligned}$$

**Threshold Selection (2 marks):**
A natural choice is the **midpoint** between the two means:

$$T = \frac{\bar{E}_{\text{healthy}} + \bar{E}_{\text{tumor}}}{2} = \frac{0.034 + 0.500}{2} = \frac{0.534}{2} = 0.267$$

Justification: This threshold is equidistant from both class means, minimizing the total misclassification rate in a 1-NN-style Bayesian sense when both classes are equally weighted.

**New MRI Classification (2 marks):**
Given reconstruction error = 0.35, since $0.35 > T = 0.267$:

$$\boxed{\text{Classification: Tumor-affected (Anomalous)}}$$

The error 0.35 is closer to the tumor mean (0.500) than the healthy mean (0.034), confirming the classification.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Biomedical ANN Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the activation function at the output layer** — Binary classification requires **sigmoid**, not softmax. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Confusing Sensitivity with Specificity** — Sensitivity = $TP/(TP+FN)$ is the **true positive rate**; Specificity = $TN/(TN+FP)$ is the **true negative rate**. Swapping them = **2 marks lost**.
> 3. **Skipping the BCE-sigmoid gradient simplification** ($\hat{y} - y$) in derivations — KTU examiners award full marks **only** when the elegant cancellation is shown explicitly.
> 4. **Omitting units / dimensions** in formula tables — Always state whether a coefficient is dimensionless, a probability, or in physical units.
> 5. **Using cross-entropy for regression problems and MSE for classification** — Both are wrong. Categorical outputs require **cross-entropy**; continuous outputs (e.g., blood pressure prediction) require **MSE/MAE**.
> 6. **Drawing CNNs as generic "black boxes"** — Always show the **conv → pool → flatten → dense** sequence. Generic diagrams = partial credit at best.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Pin This for Last-Minute Revision:**
>
> ✅ Biomedical ANN applications span **imaging, signal processing, segmentation, drug discovery, and rehabilitation**.
>
> ✅ **CNN** (U-Net, ResNet) = medical images; **RNN/LSTM/GRU** = temporal biosignals; **Autoencoder** = anomaly detection; **GAN** = data augmentation.
>
> ✅ The **sigmoid–BCE pairing** yields the elegant gradient $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial z = \hat{y} - y$ — must be derivable from scratch.
>
> ✅ **Softmax + Categorical Cross-Entropy** is the canonical multi-class diagnosis loss.
>
> ✅ **Dice Loss / DSC** is the preferred segmentation metric over pixel accuracy due to class imbalance.
>
> ✅ **Sensitivity (Recall) and Specificity** are non-negotiable clinical metrics — memorize the formulas exactly.
>
> ✅ **LSTM's three gates** — forget ($f_t$), input ($i_t$), output ($o_t$) — and the cell-state update rule are high-frequency KTU questions.
>
> ✅ **ReLU** activation dominates hidden layers for biomedical CNNs to avoid vanishing gradients.
>
> ✅ **Dropout (typically 0.5)** is essential regularization for small medical datasets.
>
> ✅ Standard pipeline: **Data acquisition → Pre-processing (filter, normalize) → Feature extraction → ANN → Post-processing → Clinical decision**.
>
> ✅ Always interpret models as **decision-support tools**, not autonomous diagnosticians — a question KTU examiners frequently raise under "ethical considerations."
